entry.content:
Somehow the artistic side of my head finds connections between this and Jesus Christ Superstar. I was in a record store the day of the tragedy, browsing. At first they had the news on, but it was too much for all of us there, I think. They then put on Jesus Christ Superstar. Amazing choice. It was just what I needed.

HEAVEN ON THEIR MINDS

(Judas)

My mind is clearer now
At last
All too well
I can see
Where we all
Soon will be
If you strip away
The myth
From the man
You will see
Where we all
Soon will be

Jesus!

You've started to believe
The things they say of you
You really do believe
This talk of God is true

And all the good you've done
Will soon be swept away
You've begun to matter more
Than the things you say

Listen Jesus
I don't like what I see
All I ask is that you listen to me
And remember
I've been your right hand man all along
You have set them all on fire
They think they've found the new Messiah
And they'll hurt you when they find they're wrong

I remember when this whole thing began
No talk of God then, we called you a man
And believe me
My admiration for you hasn't died
But every word you say today
Gets twisted 'round some other way
And they'll hurt you if they think you've lied

Nazareth's most famous son
Should have stayed a great unknown
Like his father carving wood
He'd have made good
Tables, chairs and oaken chests
Would have suited Jesus best
He'd have caused nobody harm
No one alarm

Listen Jesus, do you care for your race?
Don't you see we must keep in our place?
We are occupied
Have you forgotten how put down we are?
I am frightened by the crowd
For we are getting much too loud
And they'll crush us if we go too far
If we go too far

Listen Jesus to the warning I give
Please remember that I want us to live
But it's sad to see our chances weakening with ev'ry hour
All your followers are blind
Too much heaven on their minds
It was beautiful, but now it's sour
Yes it's all gone sour
Ah --- ah ah ah --- ah
God Jesus, it's all gone sour

Listen Jesus to the warning I give
Please remember that I want us to live
So come on, come on, listen to me.
Ah --- ah
Come on, listen, listen to me.
Come on and listen to me.
Ah --- ah

WHAT'S THE BUZZ / STRANGE THINGS MYSTIFYING

(Apostles)
What's the buzz
Tell me what's a-happening

(Jesus)

Why should you want to know?
Don't you mind about the future
Don't you try to think ahead
Save tomorrow for tomorrow
Think about today instead
I could give you facts and figures
I could give you plans and forecasts
Even tell you where I'm going

(Apostles)

When do we
Ride to Jerusalem?

(Jesus)

Why should you want to know
Why are you obsessed with fighting
Times and fates you can't defy?
If you knew the path we're riding
You'd understand it less than I




Here's the beginning of my blog. Here's what's transpired on email thusfar:

Fellow mappers:

I have been following the thread spawned by the Falwell comments with great interest. Since many of you are theologins, I thought you could help pin down the existing work on this. Here's my starting point:

From what I've seen in my experience and with this list, there is a continuum in thinking from:

Bad things as God's judgement for sin.......God's in charge and allows things.......God loves and gives us free will

I'm sure there are variations on all this, and please provide them -- things are never this linear, however I'll try to keep the diagram 2D just for my own mind's sake.

What are the texts that support any position?

I don't have any problem with the middle one, what a few emails have expressed....however I believe the far left is destructive and antithetical to love, which I see as the heart of the gospel.

The people who hold to that also usually idolize the bible, so I need to know what passages support their ideas, and which support mine. While I do not think the bible unimportant, I approach it in a different way and don't have the benefit of being able to rip old testament verses out of context to support my point of view. I need to know which verses are used to support that kind of thinking and what other verses or what context helps to refute it.

If you honestly hold to the far left there, please feel free to refute my ideas, as it will help me understand more.

Thanks all. Can you tell I want to talk to my father in law about this? :)

Daniel

PS:

John Wrote:
>I had a women
>tell me last week that it had to be all Satan's doing...
>Falwell and others like him seem to need a tangible evil to project
>their wrath upon.

Where does the "Satan's doing" fit onto graph?

Dan writes regarding the moderate/reformed view (I'm already seeing the "continuum" as a circle):
>>>>>>>>>>>
Of course this unimaginable horror stirs afresh the centuries old
theological problem of God's role in tragic disasters perpetrated
by malicious individuals. This despicable act pressures all of us
to raise serious and important questions about God and His
involvement in His universe. The overwhelming suffering and death
resulting from these terrorist acts moves us to ponder with fresh
intensity, the whole complex nature of what we call a Christian
view of reality. There are deep emotional reactions by people
who see so much evil in such an event, they want to clearly keep
God out of it. His actions come *after* such a tragedy; He stays
clear of these events before they occur. These people could not
imagine a good and loving God even remotely associated with
such evil.

There are those who see a direct relationship between the sins of
America and God's wrath. Falwell, is obviously one of them.
He was of course one of the outspoken ones to see this direct
relationship between AIDS and the judgment of God in the 80's.
I don't see how a Christian can entirely ignore this relationship for
there are a wealth of passages in the Old and New Testaments which
reveal this relationship. I know there are Christians who have great
difficulty wrestling with a view of God's love and God's wrath. For
them, this difficulty is settled by explaining the texts which portray
God's wrath as something Biblical writers perceive from their own
cultural prejudices, rather than an accurate portrayal of the God who
loves. It *seems* to me like we use one set of passages to affirm what
we think portrays God and then explain away the force of other passages
which we don't think portrays God. Of course, then, if we end up doing
it that way, could we not be also guilty of a Western cultural bias of our
own time which imposes its view of God upon a series of texts?

Coming from a reformed perspective, I don't see how we can eliminate
every trace of God's pre-involvement with a tragic event like this. I cherish
God's ultimate sovereignty over an evil act such as this. I see too many passages
which affirm God's pre-involvement over evil acts such like this. The biggest
one of course is the death of Jesus Christ. God was not asleep at the wheel
as it were, when a human being was cruelly and brutally tortured upon the
hard wood of the cross. I don't think the New Testament portrays God as
standing by the cosmic sidelines, as it were, while His Son was beaten,
scourged, and crucified by the Jewish and Roman governments. Do you
think so? Was the tragedy of Sep 11. a greater tragedy than the tragedy of
the cross? I am curious how you would answer someone who would ask you
about God's pre-involvement with the violent act of the cross?

>A lot of people I know can't get their mind around the idea of God
>NOT directing or commanding it. That is a pretty awful idea of God
>to live with.

I can't help but think that this is as much of a sweeping generalization
as Falwell's, is it not? This *appears* to be lumping me, embracing a
reformed view, with the sweeping generalization and condemnation of
Falwell. First of all, not to defend Falwell at every point, heaven forbid,
but to just acknowledge my friend, there is a prima facie case for someone
who reads the Bible, to embrace a view of God directing this or commanding
this. Would you at least agree with that observation?

Lamentations 3:32 "Though He *brings grief* (and the context reveals the nature
of this grief) he will show compassion, so great is His unfailing love."
There are scores of texts like this in the Scriptures. And it is *very plausible* to
embrace a complex theology where God *brings grief* while His love is *unfailing.*
Now, you may not embrace that theology. But are you any different than Falwell
when you start pointing your finger at those who do with such a blanket generalization
against those who do?

I have pastoral objections with Falwell's scathing generalization. I affirm the freedom
of God to judge a nation. How could I otherwise? The Bible is filled with stories
and illustrations which affirm such a reality. But I have troubles with affirming anyone
attributing an evil act with exhaustive knowledge clarity, that God is immediately
judging a nation turning towards sexual sins and secular sins. This I do struggle with.

I struggle with someone making such a comment like Falwell did without
a discernment for embracing God's heart while millions of people are suffering from
such terrible lost of life. I stand to be corrected here, but as I understand what
Falwell did in the 80's was to criticize and judge the homosexual community without
demonstrating God's heart to that suffering community. There exists a certain kind
of abstract theological musings by some with no clue about expressing God's heart to the
broken and wounded. These people may or may not have been responsible for
choosing to act in such a way as to bring a physical destruction upon them. But even
if one accepts that as a possibility it does not mean the absence of God's heart, the
absence of God's affections, the absence of God's compassions towards victims of
their own circumstances.

At this hour, with so many asking questions and wrestling with death and suffering,
I struggle with those who are not immediately pointing people to God's heart and
compassion for those who are broken. Regardless of what "label" they wore or are
wearing, the Church should be expressing God's heart towards them and pointing
them to God's heart. From a reformed perspective, theologically speaking, this event
was "ordained" (there is a loaded theological term if there ever was one) by God.
But this "ordained" relationship or mystery is not an abstract theological impersonal
decree done by a conniving and sinister God. The reformed distinction has been that
God does ordain what He also "disapproves" with His whole heart. Surely, God's
heart did not enjoy His Son dying on a cross. But this was an event planned before time.
God's decree does not always indicate God's affections or heart. Granted, there is an
incredible mystery which at some point, every believer has to acknowledge. There is
indeed, a deep complexity about the heart of God, about the plans of God, about the
being of God and the question of evil acts which we will never fathom.

However, as we sang in the chorus yesterday...
"You are the God of the broken, the friend of the weak,
You wash the feet of the weary, Embrace the ones in need."

Any theology which does not express that kind of vision of God towards those
who are hurting at this time is missing God's heart. I don't embrace what Falwell
has said. But I am not ready to throw out God's sovereignty over such an event.
<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<




Subject: Re: Talbot and Falwell

*While I do respect Talbot as a person and a musician* (please believe me!),
I was troubled by his thoroughly modern cause-and-effect search for
meta-meaning, and out of all of his comments, I must absolutely disagree
with the following:

>Lastly, there is the symbol of the airlines that were hijacked and crashed.
>This may seem a bit obscure, but it too has a lesson of repentance to
teach.
>These represent the new western lifestyle of families and businesses being
>spread our across a cities, states, and countries of a continent, and the
>continents of the world, and so many having to travel almost constantly to
>do business. This is also connected in some ways to the expansion of
>consumerism and materialism addressed above.

How can he link our expanding world culture and economy to consumerism and
materialism? The worst materialists I know are stuck right here in south
Florida because they're so busy making money that they don't have time to
even GO TO THE PARK! And some of the least materialistic/cosumeristic people
I know travel a great deal. While the tone of his entire message sat wrong
with me, this particular passage was indeed "obscure." I need to repent
because I live in Florida but my work takes me to NJ? I agree that community
sucks in this country and needs to be re-emphasized, but his comments smack
of ignorance to the realities of planet Earth circa 2001.

...Granted his "connected in some ways" is a large out to either create a
closer or more abstract connection...either way he should've kept this to
himself for a few more hours... :)

---------------
>From: Chris
>Subject: An Afghan-American Speaks:

Thanks Chris for this info. Very helpful.


>From: "Jordon
>Subject: Blame - Falwell

>I don't know how many of you have seen this but here is ABCNews.com's
article on Falwell's comments
>about the WTC. -
http://dailynews.yahoo.com/h/abc/20010916/pl/wtc_falwell010914_1.html

Thanks Jordon for this info. I am sick over it. This is exactly what I mean
by the search for meta-meaning hurting other people. I could hardly believe
the retractions at the end of the article. Clearly, they are showing hate
towards those groups with their statements, no matter what they try to say
afterward.

All: Almost right after penning my email about meta-meaning to you, I went
to my in-laws for lunch and had a very similar discussion...my
father-in-law's take on it was that the country was being judged for
rebelling against god. I argued on his ground for a while: Out of all the
countries in the world, which is the most "Christian" according to your
definition? (USA) Therefore which country would be the least likely to be
"judged" according to your logic? (USA) What about Slovakia, they are much
less "Christian" according to your definition, why aren't THEY being
"judged" this way? (they are being judged, this and that, economy) ...Got
him down to "So everything is cause and effect, our behavior causes God's
judgement/blessing? (yes, everything! {aside: this was a big deal to pin him
down to something like this, and also deeply troubling}) Then what about
Miriam's skin? Her pain is because of something she did then? (yes was the
answer, even more troubling) ...We ended by just saying, well, we'll never
believe in that, certainly not today, so let's just end it there.

Still, I sympathize with people's need for meaning.

One more thing I'm brought to in my thinking is a little story I wrote for
something we did a while back called "Creative Bible Writing." I talk about
the religious right and even use a quote from Dobson, replacing the person
he was talking about's name with Jesus'. It's helped me think about how
Jesus might have responded to these events (even though I obviously didn't
write about anything like that!...I was mirroring Bible stories from the
gospels). Here is it
http://www.geocities.com/dealingwith/bible_writing_jesus.htm

Cheers,

dealingwith




The first requirement of my psychology upon the knowledge of the events of
Sept. 11: meaning.

As I read the posts on this list of fellow journeymen and women dealing with
the same tragedy, I see that this is a common thread.

**Aside
Although I am troubled by the marginalization on this issue down age-old
lines of armenianism and calvinism, I would like to post my thoughts, as
this event has altered my thinking on meaning to a substantial degree.

Admittedly, I have consistently erred on the side of armenianism throughout
many other changes in my personal beliefs and practices. So responses like
"why did God allow this" do trouble me to some degree. However, my thoughts
on meanings do not so easily fit into these a_ or c_ stereotypes. Having a
world view that somewhat resembles what has become stereotyped as
post-modernism, I'm free to hold to both sides of the a-c "continuum" 100%.
I think meaning transcends that debate.
**/Aside

What I would like to comment on, I guess, is the apparent search for
meta-meaning among 'mappers, and I think much of the general public. Even
among terrorists.

While for me, meaning still maintains a 25% hold on my mission/vision, and
in my heart still describes a characteristic of God, my search for
meta-meaning, while already on hold to a great degree, or at least
abstracted to irrelevance, ended on Sept 11, 2001.

Justifying the death of thousands as a lesson from God to his church is, IM
not-so-humble O, unjustifiable, particularly with such little benefit of
history. Those deaths were obviously justifiable in the minds of the
perpetrators, as a lesson to all of western society and culture.

**Aside
If meta-meaning is necessary for you psychology, I guess that I just ask
that you publically air your conclusions well after the period of mourning,
which, BTW, has not even begun for some who still do not know for sure the
final fate of their loved ones. Justifying someone's loss in the light of
"greater purpose" always feels very uncompassionate, IMHO. The pursuit of
meta-meaning by 3rd parties is better left until individuals experiencing
the tragedy 1st person have had time to personally deal with it (even if in
their dealing, they use the same approach).
**/Aside

**Main Point
However, refrencing sentence #1, I am not diminishing our common need to
deal with this experience with the light of finding meaning. So here is my
first attempt at a response within this light. It is the introduction to my
next installation, and without the art that follows it may be a little
orphaned. Please read it as such:

------------
"Abuse" isn't the right word, but it's the first one that comes to mind.
See Also: Confusion
See Also: Horror
See Also: Tears
See Also: Hate

This work began as a response to the first hate mail Miriam or I have ever
received. I'm sure it will not be the last, however as the first it had a
great affect on us.

Three days later, on September 11 2001, the most terrible act of my lifetime
occurred in New York City and Washington D.C.

Without knowing how to respond to these acts of abuse and hatred on both a
micro and a macro scale, I immediately searched for meaning.

I'm convinced there is none.

This work, therefore, is about that struggle to find meaning in the face of
both small and inconceivably large pain.

There is no universal meaning for abuse, hate, terrorism, bigotry. There are
only small connections we can make each day that keep scraps of sanity in
our cupboards of life.

This work is about my attempt at a few small connections.
------------
------------

Also, thanks Bilbo for the perspective from WWI. I have wondered if part of
the terrorists' intentions is to initiate just such an event.

Cheers,

dealingwith
I just realized I made a typo in the title of this blog--I meant "face"--but fact seems like a good typo.
Welcome John Wallis to the hopeagainsthope/meaning and god in the fact of tragedy blog!!! Post your thoughts at any time, in near-real time!

Daniel,

I posted something and it is nowhere to be found. Anyway here goes.

Daniel,

The whole question of omnipotence haunts me everyday. I sat this last week and watch things that horrified me and then heard something like falwell said from many close friends and others. It all scares me. It seems though that when we followers of Christ begin to question the nature of God on this one we tremble with fear, as if even asking the question will damn us. I ask it almost everyday. I have not done an exhaustive study but have done some reading. Greg Boyd's God at War seems to be the best thing I have come accross to explain the idea of God not being omnipotent. He is expanding on the work of Pinnock and Brow and their openess of god theory. IMO pinnock and brow go too far and create an image of God that is like this big guy in the sky treating us with only love and peace. The question Boyd raises is why does God have to be omnipotent to be God. In Daniel, he prayers for God's intervention and when Michael finally arrives three weeks later, he tells Daniel that he had been detained.

Daniel 10:12-14,
Then he continued, "Do not be afraid, Daniel. Since the first day that you set your mind to gain understanding and to humble yourself before your God, your words were heard, and I have come in response to them.
But the prince of the Persian kingdom resisted me twenty-one days. Then Michael, one of the chief princes, came to help me, because I was detained there with the king of Persia.
Now I have come to explain to you what will happen to your people in the future, for the vision concerns a time yet to come."

The question that I can not get rid of why if michael knew he needed to help daniel didn't he go right away. He states that he was detained. Can God or his angels be detained? There is a never ending circle of questions and more questions that stir in my mind. I ma not sure if this is something we can or should try to answer, yet I must try. It helps me know God deeper by this quesitoning. But for some it is to much to even imagine a God that does not control everything.

Then you get into the whole evil thing. If God did not create it or can not even know it how can he use ti to judge us? If God uses evil to judge stir or kill us then he must know it. Evil is! But did God create it? I don't know. But IMO we get very close to having to say he did if we have to believe that God is omnipotent he must have at least a working knowledge of evil, if not creating it. I know this sounds like heresy to some but I think it is the logical progression of the comments made by many in the Christian world. Maybe we are attempting to understand something we can't and never will.

John

John,

We are definately trying to understand the understandable. Hence the word "god"! To me that predetermines a never ending pursuit.

The larger question is what paradigms about God are healthy and which hurtful?

I still believe in an omnipotent God, but I don't believe in a God that predetermines everything or controls every event--not even close.

Can He/She be above all and still let things go?

If He could've stopped things last week, but didn't, then is He by default "allowing" this to happen, and if you believe that, then is He by default "judging" us? I think no to both, but they are logical jumps and jumps I think many evangelicals are making. Unfortunately, the consequence of this jump is to look for the reason for the "judgment", which means judging others, which is the opposite of Love. I think Jesus made it very clear when he said he was here to love, not judge. We are apparently to follow that example!

One possible outcome in our thinking and paradigms is to accept the fact that we'll never know what God's role in terrible events like these is. He's unknowable to the fullest extent in the final analysis. Of course evangelicals would disagree, pointing to scriptures as the authoritive "word of God." That's a whole 'nother bag o' worms, however I think we can use that to prove both a)God's unknowableness (nice word, eh) (the scriptures definately say something to this effect) and b)God is not raining down rath through terrorists in 2001

If you can, insert links here to books and scripture (use Amazon and Biblegateway). Use the insert hyperlink button, which is the farthest left button of the series of buttons on the upper left (sorry if you knew that already).

BTW, I had major troubles updating a blog using a slow computer. This java takes some processing power! If yer having problems, I suggest a faster machine; if not that, then small posts *might* help, but I'm not sure....otherwise this technology seems to work pretty well. The interface is pretty good....
Oh, but my Biblegateway link didn't happen

Daniel,

I agree with the statement that we can never know God, I think at its essence God is forever unknowable. The only way we know anything of God is his/her actions towards. with and around us. For me that is what the Bible is about. I do think that this discussion is pointless at some level, because we will never know the answer. Rudolf Otto, "The Idea of the Holy" wrote a little book about this unknowability that you speak of,

I think a big part of my struggle with this whole omni issue is what it says about us, our inablility to be comfortable with the unknown and the mystery of God. I know God is and that I will never leave, but for me the pursuit of God has to be in intellectual and praxis. I think that is why I and my family have made many of the choices we have, adoption, seminary, etc. Modernity killed mystery and we must rediscover it or we too will perish as a witness to our culture. Unless we can use our intellectual ponderings with action we will be what Paul called a resounding gong, an empty voice in a cacophany of voices. I quess we are all to blame for what happened and if we are maybe God is too.

John

>>Modernity killed mystery and we must rediscover it or we too will perish as a witness to our culture.

INDEED!

So what I'm really interested in, though, is the practicality of these paradigms about God to our real lives. How do we love if we think God is judging us? Can we love only to the extent that we feel God loves us? I don't FEEL God's love towards me, but I KNOW God's love towards me, through the journey that He is creating for me to experience. But what do I do when pain and suffering and loss and failure and embarrassment are part of that journey? It's real easy for me to talk the talk when I'm having a good minute/hour/day/week/month/year, but when things are bad for ME, am I going to look for areas I need to repent in? No. I'm going to suffer the concequences. God doesn't get my attention by crashing a plane with my lover in it into the WTC. NO WAY! He got my attention once 2000 years ago, He gets my attention every day through every thing. He doesn't need to kill 5000 people to get mine or anybody's attention. He doesn't have to shout. We have to listen.

I once heard a christian comic tell a story about a really bad car accident he had when the driver fell asleep at the wheel. He said he prayed "WHY did this happen LORD?????" And God said, "Because he fell asleep at the wheel." Yes it's armenian, but how else can you frame these things? If the Lord caused it, then it has to be for our good, and there are just certain things in this world that are not for our good!!!! To read that verse any other way means you think God causes EVERYTHING. You'd have to prove that one to me another way.

Ok I don't think I stayed on topic but that's ok, dern it! :)

-Daniel

Daniel,

Why do you think you don't feel God's love toward you? Everytime you hold your wife is an expression of God's love. We were created to be that kind of creature. We have been created to love and when we do that with/to each other IMO we feel God's love. Your comment about Feeling and knowing speaks to the issue of mystery though. We have been taught not to feel. Feeling is dangerous because feeling is in the realm of the Holy Spirit and we all know how the church avoids the Spirit, well at least the evangelical branch. Feeling and knowing are things that we must stop separating. That was the crime of modernity. Modernity built a wall that created a dichotomy between feeling and knowing. I once stood looking at the Iron Curtain in rural Germany. As I stared at that never ending monument to hate and control I see now that that was what modernity did to the tie between feeling and knowing. That is why we are incapable of intimacy that may be why the attacks of last week make us all scared. We can't answer the need for a comprehensive response to the pain and doubt that filled us last week because we have to either think it through or feel it through. I think the discussion on faithmaps is evidence to that. We wanted to intellectualize the discussion; there were few expressions of heart/feeling. To become fluent in the language of mystery we need to start with a realization that feeling and knowing must be done together. Feeling without knowing results in hate or panic, knowing without feeling results in hate or panic. We need to balance the two. With all the research that points to the interconnectedness of our universe why do we still insist on speaking a language that denies the truth of who we are created to be? This relationship between feeling and knowing is essential to our unlocking our need to revell in the mystery of God. Being comfortable with the uncertainity is how we are to be. Not comfortable in the sense that we begin ot take it for granted but comfortable in knowing that that is what God's creatures are to be about. Well, so much for your discussion topic, but isn't that the point of a non-linear discussion?

Blessings
John

John,

I totally agree with everything you said! Hey! That never happens!

Let's keep getting off topic...

-D

http://groups.yahoo.com/group/faithmaps/message/2297

http://groups.yahoo.com/group/faithmaps/message/2296

http://groups.yahoo.com/group/faithmaps/message/2295

http://groups.yahoo.com/group/faithmaps/message/2293

http://www.msnbc.com/news/630551.asp?0na=23CJ3I1q

http://groups.yahoo.com/group/faithmaps/message/2290

http://groups.yahoo.com/group/faithmaps/message/2287

http://groups.yahoo.com/group/faithmaps/message/2283

Daniel, your comments about feeling and knowing inspired the following. let me know if you agrre with everything I said again, you know its much easier that way.

Modernity has committed a crime against humanity. With a measured calculated attack the modern paradigm has destroyed our sense of wonder. We have become a culture that can’t digest the mysterious. The Modern agenda sought to control and explain a world that we are just beginning to see defies understanding. The church with its need to give people formulas and doctrine has been one of the outspoken leaders of this quest. Modernity has created an image of humanity that denies the creative genius of God. Modernity spent the majority of its intellectual energy defining things that are not to be understood. The battle between the Fundamentalists and Liberals that raged in the 20th century exhausted the church’s intellect. Each camp with its measured manifesto sought to destroy the other. The prize they sought was a jewel that neither could comprehend. Each side succumbed to the desire to have its ideas as the norm. Once its agenda won out the orthodoxy that would follow would create a peaceful and calculated existence. In the quest to understand we let the rantings of a few form our image of the world and God. Control, uniformity, logic and rationality were the words we used to describe what the world was to be. Modernity obliterated our desire to be amazed. As the discoveries, theories and theology of the 20th century explicated definition and exactness something in us died. Is there any doubt why the modern church has lost its voice. Then in an instant it all seemed to change. A tidal overwhelmed the beachhead modernity had so carefully built. The 20th century is littered with ideas and formulas that are useless. The postmodern storm has destroyed all the harbors modernity used for safety. Now the battle must be taken to the internal fortresses years of denial have built.
Modernity created a dichotomy between our need to feel and our need to know. The people God created us to be needs a sense of wonder to survive. Modernity denied that need at all costs. People who would not let that spark die were pushed to the fringes of the community. Modernity above all else could support any dissention. We lived our lives by the rules this careful and banal leadership created. Socialism, Capitalism and Communism created a world where a few controlled the thoughts and desires of the many. The price we paid may never be recovered. To become fluent in the language of mystery we need to start with a realization that feeling and knowing must be done together. The revolution postmodernity has begun has awakened our spirits. We have been shown how our understanding of the world destroyed many treasures. However, our sense of mystery is the most devastated. The link between feeling and knowing is fractured. When people speak a language of mystery and wonder we need interpreters to understand. It is not that we have lost our ability to feel and know we have lost the realization that one can’t exist without the other. I once stood at the Iron Curtain in rural Germany. That monument to the modern agenda stretched to the horizon. Guard posts rose like leafless trees along its course. As I looked at the horror of a world divided I wondered who is controlling whom? Are we keeping them out or them us, or are they keeping themselves in? As this image invades my mind today it is a vivid reminder of what modernity did to our sense of wonder. Feeling and knowing separated by a never-ending fence. The horror of that crime is just beginning to sink in. For many the discussions postmodernity has brought to the public square are confusing. They are unable to see the need to connect our myriad of desires and needs. They speak a language of logic and coldness. In Acts when Peter healed the lame man the people were filled with wonder and amazement. The modern world would define the healing in medical terms or with doubt that man ever had any affliction. It was a sense of awe and wonder that allowed the Israelites to know God intimately. They did not need an explanation for the world that surrounded them they were still and knew that he was God. God never intended for us know all things. As the modern world sought answers to all things God slowly slipped from our grasps.
Our need to feel and know is part of the beautiful creatures God wants us all to become. God created in each one of us a need to feel and know. Feeling without knowing or knowing without feeling creates a lopsided image of how we are. We need to balance the two. With all the research that points to the interconnectedness of our universe why do we still insist on speaking a language that denies the truth of which we are created to be? This relationship between feeling and knowing is essential to our unlocking our need to revel in the mystery of God. This relationship is how we connect to the mystery of God. Being comfortable with the uncertainty of this mystery is one of the integral foundations for us to have our being. Not comfortable in the sense that we begin to take it for granted but comfortable in knowing that that is what God's creatures are to be about. Mystery must be celebrated. Mystery must be valued. Mystery must become the currency we use to purchase our spiritual health. Modernity killed mystery and we must rediscover it or we too will perish as a witness to our culture. If mystery is not part of our worldview then we will become a resounding gong or clanging cymbal. Without mystery we create a symphony that can’t be played. Without mystery the church as we have constructed it will die. Without mystery our souls will be polluted with images of a world that God does not know. We must embrace mystery with passion so we can once again learn the language of God. As we seek to become fluent in that language we must use our lives as metaphors to mystery. Unless we grab mystery with all our strength, mind, soul and spirit we will watch God slowly fade from our lives.

Rebecca,

I've had time to think about it now and just wonder about this point of view:

We've all had experiences where we've wanted to understand another view and tried to understand someone, but it was like banging our heads against the proverbial wall. A recent incident involving the entity known as Impact comes to mind. M and I really tried to understand Greg's POV, and we DO understand his POV--but that doesn't justify his actions, and he feels absolutely justified in those actions. No one is going to change his view of the circumstances, save God Himself. So we don't continue to associate with him anymore. Simple.

I have a feeling if the people who perpetrated these crimes had a heart to truly share their views, I think there are better ways to get our attention. In fact, if we try, as many have now, since Sept 11, to hear the views of Arab people, we may in fact hear from those voicing their concerns over the middle east and the west; from balanced persons willing to engage in dialog and mutual understanding in a non-violent manner.

The terrorists have violated the human contract that we all make with each other through our collective conscience. That conscience is challenged by the world culture we live in these days, but to drown it out completely is a perpetuation of evil. In other words, there is definately light and darkness in this world, and only those in light can communicate differing views and actual try to achieve understanding. Those in darkness must be dealt with differently, be it as simple as not interfacing with them anymore, in our micro-scale experience, or with more extreme measures (whatever that means) on this macro-scale injustice.

I agree that this country is f-d up in some major ways, has very evil elements to it. But all human endevours will.

I can't help but think this global peace and understanding idea is such a recent one. Before that, it was whoever has the biggest army wins. It's just that in the way the world has shrunk and our destructive power has grown that war has become such a disqusting thing to ponder.

One other thought. I've walked through the Arab part of Jerusalem (by accident). It was really scary. We were not welcomed. Their mind was made up about us based on the color of our skin and the clothes we wore. The same way the terrorists had made up their mind about the "west." They didn't take time to understand the lives of all the people they killed.

Please don't interpret this as eye for an eye. I just don't think the terrorists or the people that support them care about being understood or care about understanding any more than the war hawks in this country care about it. They are war hawks too. Even more determined than we are, I'm sure. They have no public opinion to win over, they have no country to keep together in the meantime. And they feel completely justified and are willing to hear nothing else.

Return questions for Daniel...

1) Just because the world was not formerly interested in global peace, does that mean it is not a lofty or godly end to seek?

2) You tried to understand Greg's point of view. We have not tried to understand theirs. We have never once brought them to the table, though we demand Israel & Palestine to meet at Camp David, and they suffer from THOUSANDS OF YEARS of similar acts. After only a hundred years (and that's being liberal) of enmity with those like Bin Laden, shouldn't we hold the same ruler to ourselves?

3) Are we not as guilty of atrocities as they? We have killed innocent people ourselves. Read today's New York Times editorial by Niall Ferguson... most of the money for IRA action comes from the U.S., and many of their actions are completely dispicable, and yet nobody comes to bomb the U.S. to make us pay up.

4) This point of view, I believe, has tried to get us to understand their point of view in many ways, and we have ignored it. The U.S. refused to even attend the latest international racism conference because the legislation was supposedly to anti-Israel... it exposed too many of their atrocities on Palestine. We only want to see one side of this battle. I, too, have entered the Palestinian section of Israel, and ate in the home of a Palestinian man when I took a class there. He told us of what it was like to have lived there the last 50 years, to have your homeland (after all, they had been there hundreds of years as well) stripped from you, to have weapons handed over to the Israelis from the U.S. as they ignored the new homeless Palestinians. To pay taxes three times the rate of Jews simply b/c of your ascent, and thus not be able to even buy land in the place you have lived your entire life. There is guilt on both sides. I am pro-Israel, but I am also pro-Palestinian. Both deserve to be heard, both have been wronged, both have been the victim and the bully. But America as a whole is only pro-Israel, and has consistently ignored the Palestinian plight, in news coverage, etc. I do not blame them for their hate. We have asked for it... we pitted them the enemy first, and refused to let their position change. They may have given up on getting us to see their point of view through civilized means, b/c those don't work. I do not condone that, but I understand it.

The only people I have ever argued violently and profusely with are my parents, and I know that it is because I knew that no "civilized" means would get their attention. I would do whatever it took to get their attention, which meant loud, angry, hateful, screaming arguments... arguments I'm not proud of, but felt cornered into. I believe they, too, are cornered.

5) "In other words, there is definately light and darkness in this world, and only those in light can communicate differing views and actual try to achieve understanding. Those in darkness must be dealt with differently, be it as simple as not interfacing with them anymore, in our micro-scale experience, or with more extreme measures (whatever that means) on this macro-scale injustice."

Can you truly communicate light by operating within darkness? I can't think of a single example where I see that in the New Testament. And have not we violated the same human contract on numerous occasion without due reason, and suffer no punishment for the acts?

6) "I just don't think the terrorists or the people that support them care about being understood or care about understanding any more than the war hawks in this country care about it. They are war hawks too." Exactly my point! We have become the monster so the monster will not break us... not a way to gain peace, in my opinion.

As I stated in my revised version, talking may do us no good, but isn't that at the very least where we should start? Do you run full force into war without first trying every other avenue, even if you are unsure if it will dead end? I cannot do that with a clear conscience... cannot have my boyfriend (or me- equality for women, right?) drafted to serve in a 10-year war when I know that every other civilized avenue was not FIRST tried... cannot see thousands more die when the simplest (though hardest) solution was never sought....

R

MILITARY HISTORIAN SPEAKS

An academic advisor at the Air Force Academy wrote the following,
which we believe everyone possible should read.

From: Dr. Tony Kern, Lt Col, USAF (Ret)

Recently, I was asked to look at the recent events through the lens
of military history. I have joined the cast of thousands who have
written an "open letter to Americans."


Dear friends and fellow Americans 14 September, 2001

Like everyone else in this great country, I am reeling from last week's
attack on our sovereignty. But unlike some, I am not reeling from
surprise. As a career soldier and a student and teacher of military
history, I have a different perspective and I think you should hear it.
This war will be won or lost by the American citizens, not diplomats,
politicians or soldiers.

Let me briefly explain.

In spite of what the media, and even our own government is telling us,
this act was not committed by a group of mentally deranged fanatics. To
dismiss them as such would be among the gravest of mistakes. This attack
was committed by a ferocious, intelligent and dedicated adversary. Don't
take this the wrong way. I don't admire these men and I deplore their
tactics,
but I respect their capabilities. The many parallels that have been made
with the Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor are apropos. Not only because it
was a brilliant sneak attack against a complacent America, but also because
we may well be pulling our new adversaries out of caves 30 years after we
think this war is over, just like my father's generation had to do with
the formidable Japanese in the years following WW II.

These men hate the United States with all of their being, and we must
not underestimate the power of their moral commitment. Napoleon, perhaps
the world's greatest combination of soldier and statesman, stated "the
moral is to the physical as three is to one." Patton thought the Frenchman
underestimated its importance and said moral conviction was five times more
important in battle than physical strength. Our enemies are willing --
better said anxious -- to give their lives for their cause. How committed
are we America? And for how long?

In addition to demonstrating great moral conviction, the recent attack
demonstrated a mastery of some of the basic fundamentals of warfare taught
to most military officers worldwide, namely simplicity, security and
surprise. When I first heard rumors that some of these men may have been
trained at our own Air War College, it made perfect sense to me. This was
not a random act of violence, and we can expect the same sort of military
competence to be displayed in the battle to come. This war will escalate,
with a good portion of it happening right here in the good ol' U.S. of A.

These men will not go easily into the night. They do not fear us. We must
not fear them. In spite of our overwhelming conventional strength as the
world's only "superpower" (a truly silly term), we are the underdog in this
fight. As you listen to the carefully scripted rhetoric designed to prepare
us for the march for war, please realize that America is not equipped or
seriously trained for the battle ahead. To be certain, our soldiers are much

better than the enemy, and we have some excellent "counter-terrorist"
organizations, but they are mostly trained for hostage rescues, airfield
seizures, or the occasional "body snatch," (which may come in handy). We
will
be fighting a war of annihilation, because if their early efforts are any
indication, our enemy is ready and willing to die to the last man.
Eradicating
the enemy will be costly and time consuming. They have already deployed
their
forces in as many as 20 countries, and are likely living the lives of
everyday
citizens. Simply put, our soldiers will be tasked with a search and destroy
mission on multiple foreign landscapes, and the public must be patient and
supportive until the strategy and tactics can be worked out.

For the most part, our military is still in the process of redefining itself

and presided over by men and women who grew up with - and were promoted
because they excelled in - Cold War doctrine, strategy and tactics. This
will
not be linear warfare, there will be no clear "centers of gravity" to strike

with high technology weapons. Our vast technological edge will certainly be
helpful, but it will not be decisive. Perhaps the perfect metaphor for the
coming battle was introduced by the terrorists themselves aboard the
hijacked
aircraft -- this will be a knife fight, and it will be won or lost by the
ingenuity and will of citizens and soldiers, not by software or smart bombs.

We must also be patient with our military leaders.

Unlike Americans who are eager to put this messy time behind us, our
adversaries have time on their side, and they will use it. They plan
to fight a battle of attrition, hoping to drag the battle out until the
American public loses its will to fight. This might be difficult to believe
in this euphoric time of flag waving and patriotism, but it is generally
acknowledged that America lacks the stomach for a long fight. We need only
look as far back as Vietnam, when North Vietnamese General Vo Nguyen Giap
(also a military history teacher) defeated the United States of America
without ever winning a major tactical battle. American soldiers who marched
to war cheered on by flag waving Americans in 1965 were reviled and spat
upon
less than three years later when they returned. Although we hope that Usama
Bin Laden is no Giap, he is certain to understand and employ the concept.

We can expect not only large doses of pain like the recent attacks, but also

less audacious "sand in the gears" tactics, ranging from livestock
infestations to attacks at water and food supplies and power distribution
facilities.

These attacks are designed to hit us in our "comfort zone" forcing the
average American to "pay more and play less" and eventually eroding our
resolve. But it can only work if we let it. It is clear to me that the will
of the American citizenry - you and I - is the center of gravity the enemy
has targeted. It will be the fulcrum upon which victory or defeat will turn.

He believes us to be soft, impatient, and self-centered. He may be right,
but if so, we must change. The Prussian general Carl von Clausewitz, (the
most often quoted and least read military theorist in history), says that
there is a "remarkable trinity of war" that is composed of the (1) will of
the people, (2) the political leadership of the government, and (3) the
chance and probability that plays out on the field of battle, in that order.

Every American citizen was in the crosshairs of last Tuesday's attack, not
just those that were unfortunate enough to be in the World Trade Center or
Pentagon.

The will of the American people will decide this war. If we are to win, it
will be because we have what it takes to persevere through a few more hits,
learn from our mistakes, improvise, and adapt. If we can do that, we will
eventually prevail.

Everyone I've talked to in the past few days has shared a common
frustration,
saying in one form or another "I just wish I could do something!" You are
already doing it. Just keep faith in America, and continue to support your
President, the military, and their decisions the outcome is certain. If, on
the other hand, we lose our faith, our resolve and fail to do so, the
outcome
is equally certain.

God Bless America

/s/ Dr. Tony Kern, Lt Col, USAF (Ret)
Former Director of Military History
U.S. Air Force Academy

Hi all,

All the discussion about the reaction/response for the attacks has had us mentioning grace alot. Now that is topic I know very little about in a tangible way. How do we embody grace in a world that relishes hate. Each time I hear about something that seems to project grace I doubt it, too much cultural programming I guess. I do have many experiences of grace though. Our two younest kids Isaiah and Micah were abandoned in J'burg South Africa. Isaiah was found in a dumpster, which probably saved his life becuase it was very cold when they found him. Micah was left in a park and most likely found quickly. For me the fact that each of these boys who may have most likely died have been given a life. By coming to be part of our family, the people who found them, the people who cared for them in SA it is all examples of embodied grace. If we allow ourselves to be open to the Spirit and act in passion and boldness I believe we can embody grace. All we can control about our life is how we react and act towards others and their actions. I think when I ponder the last days I need to be more open to grace than I let myself. Not sure this is going anywhere but what do y'all think.

John
IndyMedia Center - NYC

Hi all,

Just wanted to share something with you. We have heard much about how kids have processed all this death and destruction. I was just praying with my three year old and his pray was that all the people who died would be made alive again so their families would not have to miss them anymore. It struck me how my little boy is so open to God that he thinks if he asks all those people will return to their families. I know it will never happen in this world, but as Creed says "But, my friend, I'd sacrifice all those nights If I could make the Earth and my dreams the same" Someday I will realize that peace. power, release of pain, unending worship and reveling in God's presence. Noah's words made my spirit soar as I thought about what is to come. I know it seems hopeless in the midst of this chaos, but we are given glimpses of what is to come. The deja vu of heaven, something we already know deep in our spirits. It is that hope that sustains me in times of despair and pain. Yet, I relish the fight here and the small victories achieved like the innonce of my son's heart. May God bless us all as we struggle and live and breath and have our being in this momentary place that is not our home.

Blessings
John

Hey Rebecca, I didn't mean to stop talking about the politics of the issues, I just meant that I wasn't educated or emotionally involved enough intelligently speak to those issues. I would love if you continued.

On an issue where I am so involved, today I watched on tv the memorial service at Yankee Stadium. If there were a definition of true worship that would be it. If in simply the power of unity amongst diversity. I have rarely been so moved by a "service" yet alone one I'm watching on the television. I think that the experience speaks volumes about true worship.

I was also thinking that we all make our own worship through the meanings, and the activities that create those meanings, that we develop and hold to through our own experience and our own dealing with {fill in the blank}.

I was thinking of the last little "worship experience" I created, first for myself, then shared with those close to me. It had in its very nature a nature of difficulty because the spiritual nature of it was so abstract. Looking at the results certainly attests to that. However when the spirituality is abstracted to such a degree, it is easy to miss the point of transcendence or reverence...and while humor and fun is certainly a part of life and therefore worship, there is a point where humor moves to sarcasm moves to veiled attack. In some other words, there was a meaning and a purpose behind what we did, and some people, even if they did not connect intellectually with it, they connected on a spiritual level with it ...while others did not look for spiritual significance.

I have no idea where I'm going with that...

Except to say that I think worship is going to become more and more integrated and diverse, or I should say our realization of worshipful experiences or reverent transcendence, much like the memorial service today in NYC. And for me, my art is that reverent transcendence, "seeking :: stories :: beauty :: meaning" really does encapsulate all that I am attempting to create. To capture a small piece of the infinate Creator. But how do I realize that in light of a spiritually dull world?

...aside: in that vein, I am finding myself more and more drawn to the artist community at large. And it turns out that homosexuals are more predominant within that community than they are within the community at large. While for some time I have perferred the company of homosexuals because they actually demonstrate Godly characteristics like unconditional love, etc., the emotional depths that a true artist much reach is making for, while not gay sexually, a much more emotionally androgenous dealingwith. I also think this is due to a marriage where each of us are equal partners, where there is no hierarchy, is to cause for some of this. Many would have a problem with all the previous statements, and "hate mail" is a response to someone who obviously felt I must be dickless to want to pursue art with my life. However it is those close to a spiritual center described by not just those four words of djdc's tenent, but by many qualities--qualities unfortunately absent from much of the world, particularly in the Christian world.

...and in the vein of what to do with our lives; as Miriam and I continue to struggle with that; I thought today that such a crisis as these last 12 days have been for all of us, what we turn to really demonstrates what is closest to our hearts: I turned to my art, in this case my already in-process installation preperations, "hate mail;" for Rebecca, political activism and a realization that she is indeed a closet lobbyist (sp?). For John (please any of you correct me if I'm off) a refocus on the true qualities of God through the lens of his experience with family, specifically a multi-racial/cultural family and lifestyle.

...and in light of my thing-that-is-closest-to-my-heart, I realized (even though it's been told plain to my face already) that being an artist has nothing to do with the art you create, but rather on a decision on how you spend your life. Almost every great artist, save a small percentage of "pop" artists (particularly those in "pop" entertainment--music and film and most rarely literature), lives a poor life. The phrase starving artist doesn't exist for nothing! But in the lens of history their contribution is great. {highly recommend the movie "Pollock"}

Note on blogger bugs: Rebecca et al, what I've been doing is copying what I've typed to the clipboard (cntrl+C) real quick before I hit post or post&publish (I realized there should be no difference between the two, one simply sends one command to the server, the other 2 commands, but one after the other). That way if it blows up, I can just paste it all back into this window and try again.

Daniel,

Art, mystery, worship have all been destroyed by modernity to some extent. Art has become "pop" for many, just another commodity. Your talk about exploring worship or connecting to the transedent is exciting. I think about the time, money and passion that was put into the work of artist in past ages. The murals on/in buildings, the paintings, the scuplture that dripped with theological meaning. How Christ or any other imge was placed or used carried great weight for the artist and the viewers of the finished work. We have been dulled to the point that nothing seems beautiful. Our senses know only mediocrity. When I look at the icons Eastern Orthodoxy uses in their worship at times I am blown away. But then I am smacked in the face with my world, where beauty is fleeting. I believe we are entering into the age of the Holy Spirit. The way we know the HS is through symbol, the langauge of symbol will become essential to the church as we try to express faith in the future. I am not speaking of literal symbols like the cross, but subtle ones. Ones like how you embrace a friend, can speak volumes about how you value them. Your quest to find meaning in things that you create or find is wonderful. We need to have our souls awakened to the beauty that permeates everything that surrounds us. I mentioned once about expereinceing God in traffic. What I meant was that a thing that we have created, ie rush hour, can be a place where we encounter the divine if we allow ourselves to let it happen. There sitting in traffic surrounded by people I don't know I am overcome with the beuaty of our diverse world. In a situation totally out of my control I am struck with the immensity of God and the fraility of me. Passing a wreck the twisted metal and shattered plastic speak to the unlimited value of life and our utter failure in trying to control it. So keep going brother and always remember everywhere you go God has been there before, during and after you. The landscape of SFLA with its bleached out patina is filled with beauty and meaning. The church has accepted the world the way it is and what we need to be doing is training explorers that are fearless in the pursuit of meaning, beauty, etc. It is like we are all standing on the edge of a cliff and across that chasm there are a few people running around searching for something and we have no idea how they got over there, maybe they have been there all the time and we have been unable or unwilling to see them. Some of us know we need to be on the other side, but are afraid to ask the ones already there for help. Some of us are jumping across and not making it overcome with the desire to be in front others are constructing walls to block our view of the other side. Then some of us are looking over with loging and a desire to take others across with us to build a new world. How do we build a bridge that crosses that void without the help of the ones already there and also with those who wish to stay behind. I want to be part of those who build the bridge and the most rewarding part of the task is the building not what we discover when we get across. Although the world over there will amaze us in its beauty and complexity and simplicity. We need to build now or we will lose our passion and desire and become once again like the church in the 20th century, afraid of what is coming and determined to build a world that denies reality. "seeking :: stories :: beauty :: meaning" is a way you and others can invite people to walk accross the bridge we are building together.

John

Hey is anyone still out there? I have been swamped with life so have not benn able to post anything. So whats up with y'all? I have been writing feverishly the ideas won't stop coming, help me i'm drowning. Here is soemthing i did a few days ago let me know what you think. I have been thinking about how we won't embrace mystery as a church and how much we miss because of that.

Rediscovering a God of Mystery

While attending a study on Revelation my wife was confused by a comment made by the leader. As part of a discussion about Jesus Christ the comment was made that not all aspects of God were present in the embodied Jesus. As my wife and I discussed the implications of that simple comment I was reminded of our never-ending quest to explain away mystery. The fact that Jesus Christ was both a person and God at the same time is something we can’t explain. Colossians 1:19-20 states, “For in him all the fullness of God was pleased to dwell, and through him to reconcile to himself all things, whether on earth or in heaven, making peace by the blood of his cross.” How did all God’s fullness dwell within a person? Can we ever understand something so amazing? Do we need to understand to make our faith complete?

Our world wants to explain and rationalize everything and we have become accustomed to knowing how to do just that. From an early age we are programmed to value knowledge and logic. We stress the importance of being able to create and design theories, philosophies and doctrines that celebrate the scientific and orderly explication of the creation that surrounds us. We need scientific theories to ease the tension in our minds. A tension that is caused by the very nature of what we are created to be. When the great I AM saw fit to create our world and place people at its center God created a being that was limited. The minds that we have been given are not capable of explaining all things. Yet, we have spent most of our energy over the past centuries attempting to explain God, the Bible and creation with rationality and logic. None of these things can be quantified in the ways we have been trying to do for so long. The Bible and its collection of stories is meant to inspire and stir our sense of wonder. Stories like that of the creative love of God in Genesis were never intended to be a scientific theory about how God went about creating our complex world.

Many believe that if you read Genesis as something other than a logical and precise treatise on creation you deny God. However, what if Bernhard W. Anderson in his book, From Creation to New Creation : Old Testament Perspectives is right and Genesis is to be read as a parable. What if that amazing story was written while the Israelites were in exile? Foreigners in a strange land in need of comfort and understanding about their place in God’s creation. The power of the story takes on a whole new focus. God using the eloquence of an inspired authors tells the people chosen to be the nation of God that they are exactly that. A people, who have been living with a sense of emptiness, subjected to an existence of uneasiness and rootlessness. The power of the story inspiring a people whom are about to rediscover the essence of their identity. Amazed by the understanding that the God that created all things has singled out this people to tell the world the story of whom God is and how God is going to complete the work started long ago.

A covenant begun with Abraham carried on through the ages and now being redefined for a people whom about to embark on the greatest journey in history. Imagine being told that you are part of a mystery that was designed to reconcile all things to God. Being taught that the mystery that created the world is about to use you as an integral part of that creating. God who created out of nothing a world, a universe and a people who are part of a continuing expression of creative love is telling you that you are the ones to carry out that mission. God never ceasing in the desire to bring the message of love and transformation to an unending succession of generations. Imagine being oppressed, beat down and subjected to an ever present evil and then hearing that you the ones your world says are nothing are God’s chosen people. Dispersed and disconnected yet loved and chosen for a holy and essential journey that will bring all people under the fullness of God.

The power of the story has been ignited by the surroundings of those who are hearing it for the first time. In a world where the written word is absent for most people the power of this story takes on a life that begins a rebirth of a nation. A nation that was destroyed because they had forgotten the very essence of their creation. God, the ever-present power is telling these people who have ignored and abandoned their identity that he has never forgotten who they are. Imagine the sense of wonder that overtakes the minds, hearts and souls of the Israelites. Believing and living as if you have been discarded and then understanding that that was never true. The mystery of why God has done this would be unimaginable. It would make no sense upon the first hearing, yet as the story and its power began to inhabit the souls of the people their view of themselves and their world change.

The slow and sometimes painful process of changing the image you have created to protect yourself from a world that denies you any dignity beginning in the telling of a story. Starting to make the connection with the history that you have known since childhood, Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Joseph, Moses, Joshua, David, Solomon, Rehobaom, Asa, Omri, Josiah and a thread of success and failure that has followed you since the beginning. Understanding that God is doing something that only God could do, yet using you a small and unworthy creature to do it. How could you help but be overwhelmed by the mystery of what God is doing? How could you explain it in rational and logical terms?

Yet, that is exactly what the church has been doing since the cross. We have lost our connection with the mystery that is the essence of God. Succumbing to our need to explain all things in terms that can be quantified and rationalized, but in the end destroying our own heritage. As we stand staring into a whirlwind that is our world we must rediscover the mystery of God. We must read stories like the ones in Genesis as parables. We must once again embrace a God that we can’t and will not explain. In our experiment to control an uncontrollable creation we have destroyed the very thing we need to survive. Without a desire to revel and celebrate the mystery of God we have lost our connection with the witness and testimony of all those who have come before us. Mystery is essential to a faith that connects with the divine. As we attempt to construct an expression of faith we must do all we can to regain the desire to accept the unexplainable and in that accepting begin to hear the voices from our past. Those voices are screaming from every crevice of creation that God can not be explained. As we journey forward to the completion of God’s unending creative genius, mystery must be something we embrace with passion and tenacity. As the psalmist cries out, “Our God is in the heavens; he does whatever he pleases,” we must stop explaining away the mystery that is the essence of God. By embracing the mystery that is God we will rediscover who we really are in a world that would have us believe a different story. Our story is one of reveling and celebrating the things we can’t explain. People who do not know God will begin to see our embrace of mystery as something that they want to be a part. Rational and logical explanations of God litter the past centuries what the church needs to do is articulate an understanding and acceptance of mystery that intrigues a world that denies that very mystery. Once we see God the way the Israelites in exile did we will begin to reach our world and God’s essence and power will transform all things.

Thinking about submitting ot next wave.

Hey John. Thanks for submitting some material! Don't worry if it is a few days between posts here; this is just for us, for when the inspiration hits.

I like the concepts you discuss here. One thing I've thought about doing a lot lately is explaining the concepts I'm talking about using the concepts I'm talking about. In other words, you're talking about mystery and story, two topic very dear to my heart; so talk about them with a mysterious story. This instead of trying to convince the rationalists of mystery and story with a rational-critical work. In other words, don't fight fire with fire.

Cheers,

Where Are You, Mrs. Miniver?

Thanks rebecca!

Hey folks,

It's Linford here with a quick update for you all.

First, Karin sends her thanks for the heartfelt words of encouragement that
so many of you took time to send regarding her mother's health. They meant
more than you know. Look for a longer update from Karin as things continue to
progress. Karin's mother is still trying to regain her speech and the use of
the right side of her body, following the brain hemmorhage, but she is in
stable condition, and continues to make tiny improvements. She has been
transferred to Cincinnati, and it has been a very difficult time, but we
continue to hope for the best. Immense thanks again for your thoughts and
prayers.

I did do some touring with Cowboy Junkies in September in order to help pay a
few band bills, but I was home just in time to celebrate wedding anniversary
numero five. So in spite of a lot of what's been happening, life has had its
good moments during these blustery October days. Indian summer. Crows
gathering in the trees.

We were deeply saddened and of course stunned by the tragic events that
unfolded in NYC, Washington DC and Pennsylvania on September 11. Like so many
artists and musicians, we were at a loss for words. We too had to ask
ourselves, What place does art and music have in times like these?

Our hope is that we can somehow participate in tiny redemptive acts in the
coming months and years, that may (even if in ways embarrassingly small) help
to heal this broken world of ours. When any of us encounters beauty, we
subconsciously dream of a more perfect world, the door is flung wide on new
possibilities that we can't yet imagine, we breathe more deeply and wonder if
we have more to offer than we know. Although I couldn't imagine making my own
music for quite some time after September 11, the music of others held me at
times and was a comfort somehow.

While our government launches the bombing campaign in Afghanistan, we are
troubled by many conflicting feelings. My heritage is one of pacificism, but
it seems presumptuous to wax eloquent about loving one's enemies when I enjoy
the relative safety and prosperity of a land well-guarded. It is the duty of
governments, I suppose, to protect themselves and their people. I do believe
if the men flying those planes on the 11th could have killed 5,000,000
instead of 5000 they would have done so. It's unsettling to know that there
are people who would happily eradicate Western Civilization. And yet as we
start blowing things up in Afghanistan, it is inevitably the poor who will
suffer, families huddled along the sides of roads, and it just feels so damn
archaic somehow. With all the intelligence and resources that we have
available to us, couldn't we help the world invent a different way? And yet
it seems there are many (even in Washington) who are struggling to see the
big picture.

Well, I'm sure you've all been thinking some of these same things. As usual
when it comes to things that matter, there are no easy answers. Our thoughts
and prayers continue to be with the victims and their families.

Moving on to things Over the Rhine for the time being, again, in spite of
everything, we are going to go forward with our tour of Europe. We feel it's
the right thing to do, and we will have the opportunity to introduce our
music (God willing) to thousands of new people. Hopefully our guardian angels
will have a relatively uneventful trip apart from a lot of sightseeing and
some unusually inspired music.

And finally, I have another update on my book project. We had to cancel about
30 shows recently to deal with a family crisis, and I was left with a book
project that I suddenly couldn't pay for. I pulled the plug on plan "A". In
the meanwhile, I was absolutely delighted and surprised when a small press in
Kentucky that I have admired from a distance for years, offered to print a
limited run of my project. Larkspur Press has been making beautiful,
handmade, letterpress books, and chapbooks for years. I had purchased several
of their editions of Wendell Berry poems or short stories, and had always
thrilled to see their commitment to fine bookmaking. I'll be meeting with
them later this year, and I can't believe my luck, frankly, that they are
willing to be involved.

Now the thing is, they are all Luddites at heart down at Larkspur, and they
warned me that they don't pay attention to deadlines. All they would commit
to is getting me my book sometime next year! I, for one, am willing to wait,
and eager to see the result of their efforts, whenever that may be.

Karin asked me the other night how many had pre-ordered my book during the
little special we ran back in June. I said, Oh, about 250 people. She said,
That's definitely enough for an angry mob.

Well put.

Here is where we are: This book is going to be limited to a run of 500
copies. If you pre-ordered one last June, some time next year you will
receive your signed and numbered Larkspur Press edition. If you would like to
get your money back because of the delay, just e-mail Stacie at
otrhine@aol.com, and she will send you a check for $15.00 and take you off
the list.

I'm guessing that, at the end of the day, most people won't mind waiting, so
let's assume that 250 are spoken for. I would like to give 100 to family and
friends, so that leaves 150 that are still available. These will sell for $15
plus $3 shipping, and we will be putting them up on the website catalog soon.
If you would like to reserve a copy, you can do so while they last. Because
there are a limited number, I'll go ahead and sign and number these as well.

(For those that pre-ordered, to buy Grey Ghost Stories and the book
separately costs $36 with shipping, so you saved $11. We do apologize for the
change in plans.)

And last but not least, after an unforeseen two month break, we are thrilled
to be doing a few intimate shows this wknd. Friday, we're playing at Brady's
in Kent, Ohio, and Saturday, we're playing in the tiny town of Dalton, Ohio,
a stone's throw from Kidron, which is more or less in the heart of Amish
country. You can catch the Amish flea market and cattle auction during the
day in Kidron, buy a few heifers to feed over the winter, and then hear our
own unique brand of sorrow and love flowing mingled down that evening in
Dalton. It's part of a new concept we're working on: weekend getaways with
Over the Rhine. Wonder of wonders, my parents will be attending this show.
Anything could happen.

(Brady's is the sort of warm, glowing establishment that Frodo, Sam, Pippin
and Merry would have loved after several days of tiresome trekking.)

Go to overtherhine.com for all the pertinent info. We hope to see you.

Until then, be brave and free and inspired,

Linford Detweiler





On a side note, for what it's worth, never have we come so close to pulling
the plug on the Over the Rhine discussion list as when someone named Chris
Emery, made a flip comment immediately following the acts of terror that was
utterly beyond the pale of human decency in our opinion, whatever his leg
itimate political concerns may have been. While thousands were scrambling for
news of family members in the wreckage, and while the Jay Leno's of the world
had the good sense to shut up for once, Chris opted to use his computer
keyboard to perpetrate his own unique brand of divisiveness and contempt for
the feelings of others. I opted not to send my response at the time in a
hardwon moment of restraint, and was comforted with the impassioned hope that
he may not really be a fan of our music, rather someone who hangs around on
the discussion list because he is cynical and lacks options.
In response to those who wrote us directly, those of us associated with Over
the Rhine were outraged and embarrassed to be associated in some way with his
comments which we found to be ill-timed and frankly, sickening.

The discussion list was intended to be a wide open forum for the discussion
of Over the Rhine's music. It's not moderated, and we may move to a different
format in coming months. Much of the tone and content of the discussion list
is not all that relevant to what Over the Rhine is about (according to the
great majority of our fans), but there have been some great moments, however
few and far between. The band has always taken a hands off approach with the
list, but I guess if someone wishes to use that pallette to express
sentiments that are a blatant effront to the spirit of Over the Rhine and the
community we've tried to bring together over the years, they'll probably hear
from us sooner or later. Maybe we've been silent too long. Enough said.

I'm glad some of you appreciated the humor of my characterization of the iconic ooze dude.
