Kit, it's a really long story about me and the Episc church. I stopped going as soon as my parents stopped expecting/forcing me to go. When I went to college I became a super-right charis-fundie jerk. I was reformed 4 years ago and currently head up the FA chapter in my area. There is currently no church that I could attend with joy ('tho the next time I go to a 'service' will probly be Episc w/ the folks next time we're together). I do my own things and invite my friends or go to 'real world' spiritual events like concerts and art galleries. But, I mostly left the Episc church because it was my parent's church, you know? When you break it down, people go to church for entertainment and/or tradition and/or social connectedness (and oh yeah, spiritual stuff on occasion, too). I was basically the only young person at that church and wasn't mature enough to create my own meanings from the structure that was there. 10 years later I might have a different approach. I think the Episc church has possibly the greatest potential of all existing church institutions. Many vibrant young communities have grown out of Anglican traditions in the UK (oft mentioned on this list Dave Tomlinson is an Anglican priest now; the vaux community is also out of an Anglican church {i think}). IMHO, the main thing with a litergical tradition is that there is so much meaning behind the acts of worship that is never expressed or communicated. Instead of being a framework for interface with God that one can approach and use from within their own context, it was for me a rote. Instead of being a gardener, I was just the hired plowman. Not only could I not bring my own context as a young person to it, there was no context for me to place around it.

Howdy,
Just got to say that that was a bunch of crap. I know you don’t write them but I assume you read them.
What is “His country?”
To whom did we supply arms to that attack his country? Russia? I don’t think so. Iraq and the host of others that surround them are all sold arms by Russia. Now we are supplying arms to anyone willing to fight the terrorists. I whole heartedly support that. I do not support killing innocent children or “wiping Them off the face of the earth” but what are we to do with a nation of children that has been brainwashed from an early age to hate Americans?
I shudder to think. The amazing thing is, as far as the terrorism is concerned, left and right are united. Sure there are people like the person that wrote that little satire. I pity our nation and the world should we take on that train of thought.
All I can say is, thank God that George Bush is president. I know that probably sets you off but I can’t even imagine what Al would be doing right now. Under the bed in the Lincoln bedroom no doubt. Just kidding of course but you get my drift. Had the former president spent more time chasing Binny and less time chasing bootie, we might not be in this situation. (ouch!)
I have to ask myself why it takes such a tragic event to bring out the “Best” in all of us. Unfortunately, I have to ask why it also brings out the worst. You may know, Boca Raton is the next city from Delray Beach. I have no doubt that the events of this past week here will be played out across the nation in the coming months. It saddens me. I do not believe that these have anything to do with what happened on the 11th of last month. These scares have been going on all over the country for years. Unfortunately someone has procured the “real” virus for these attacks.
Are we as Americans wrong in trying to help an economically depressed nation to rebuild after a decade of war or to feed their starving children? Should people be sentenced to death for preaching Christianity? This is what the radical Muslims are doing in many countries. I am sure you have read of the way they treat women. What should we do? Become isolationists? Little Bush was accused of this in recent months after visiting and hosting more leaders in the first 6 months of his presidency than Clinton did in his first 3 years.
As a Christian, I was appalled at the comments of Jerry Falwell and the agreement of Pat Robertsen. To say that what happened on September 11th was God’s vengeance upon our nation for supporting Gays and Lesbians and abortion is simply put “Absurd.” They do not represent the true Christian faith in anyway. If that is the “Religious Right” then I am sorry to say, “They are wrong.”
My God is a loving God. He has taught me to hate the sin but love the sinner. It is not up to me to condemn but to forgive. While I am against abortion it is not my right to pass judgment. It is His (Hers)
I can forgive the acts brought against us but I cannot excuse them. The perpetrators must be either eliminated or brought to justice. Preferably the latter. It will only add more fuel to the fire to indiscriminately kill those that offend not only our nation but the nations of the world.
As far as Gays and Lesbians go, (no pun intended) that is their freedom. Who am I to discriminate against them? I have had many friends and acquaintances who were Gay or Lesbian. Does God love them any less? I doubt it.
I think our politicians have learned a valuable lesson in taking sides with only our interests in mind but we must always stride to bring peace to the world. Peace, Love and Freedom are the very foundations of any relationship, be it personal or otherwise. Countries that suppress their population should be held accountable. They are not interested in the well-being of their country but of their own self interests.
We are a nation of individuals. All races, faiths, sexual orientation and ethnic backgrounds are welcome. Some say that these freedoms unconstrained. How can freedom be exercised otherwise?

This excellent response from jordan cooper
Yahoo! Groups : faithmaps Messages :Message 3262 of 3283

Good News/Bad News
From "The Astonished Heart: Reclaiming the Good News from the Lost-and-Found of Church History", Wm B Eerdmans Publishing Co. 1996

On, then, with a look at the new models of the church currently on the market. ...

MODELS WE'RE ALREADY BUYING:
The New Corporate Model;
The Mega-Church Model;
The Professionalism-of-the-Clergy Model;
The Renewal Models, comprising
the Church-Growth movement,
the Spiritual/Charismatic Model,
and the Cursillo/Marriage-Encounter Model;
The Church-as-Advocacy-Group Model


MODELS WE HAVE YET TO CONSIDER:
The Alcoholics Anonymous Model;
The Marginal-Church Model

The Mega-Church Model (meets California emission standards, but can't stop at the edge of a cliff). Like Jeremiah speaking to Israel before the Captivity, I have not a kind word to say for it. Its sheer size makes it corporately successful in the extreme—angelic to a fare-thee-well. Its consumerism is flagrant. It sells what the market demands: religion, not Gospel; goods and services, not confrontation; life enhancement, not redeeming death. Besides, it's far too close to being the entertainment industry at prayer. It provides prime-time nursery care (complete with big-screen TV messages to summon the parents of intractable children), gymnasiums for "Christian aerobics," mini-dramas and sitcoms during services, inhouse bookstores and T-shirt marts ("the church-front store"), feelgood songs with sticky lyrics and marshmallow melodies—and, above all, the consolations of soda-pop theology. In short, it's WalMart from start to finish: it stocks only what sells and doesn't give shelf-room to what doesn't. But size and numbers guarantee nothing except the lowest common denominator of everything. Remember television? When there were just three networks, NBC had a symphony orchestra and CBS had Captain Kangaroo—and even then we called it a wasteland. Now we have fifty-odd channels, and Melrose Place is all over the place. So what do you think we're going to have when there are five hundred channels? Quality programming? Don't hold your breath. You haven't seen from wasteland yet.

But the last and saddest thing about the mega-church is the rest of the churches' rush to imitate it—and their almost universal unhappiness at not being able to. It may be the Home Shopping Network on its knees, but it's not the church. It may cater to a clientele large enough to make the mainline churches green with envy; but if you've got a good little restaurant, why should you waste your time being jealous of the fast-food chains? The mega-church isn't catholic, it's just big; it's no model for an outfit with a God who thinks small is the cat's meow—and who has regularly given flat tires to every stretch limo the church has historically tried to drive. So thank you very much, Mr. Dealer, but no thanks. Megachurch? No sale.

Relativism, postmodernism, or just plain understanding?....

From the NY Times:

October 15, 2001

Condemnation Without Absolutes
BY STANLEY FISH


CHICAGO -- During the interval between the terrorist attacks and the United States response, a reporter called to ask me if the events of Sept. 11 meant the end of postmodernist relativism. It seemed bizarre that events so serious would be linked causally with a rarefied form of academic talk. But in the days that followed, a growing number of commentators played serious variations on the same theme: that the ideas foisted upon us by postmodern intellectuals have weakened the country's resolve. The problem, according to the critics, is that since postmodernists deny the possibility of describing matters of fact objectively, they leave us with no firm basis for either condemning the terrorist attacks or fighting back.

Not so. Postmodernism maintains only that there can be no independent standard for determining which of many rival interpretations of an event is the true one. The only thing postmodern thought argues against is the hope of justifying our response to the attacks in universal terms that would be persuasive to everyone, including our enemies. Invoking the abstract notions of justice and truth to support our cause wouldn't be effective anyway because our adversaries lay claim to the same language. (No one declares himself to be an apostle of injustice.)

Instead, we can and should invoke the particular lived values that unite us and inform the institutions we cherish and wish to defend.

At times like these, the nation rightly falls back on the record of aspiration and accomplishment that makes up our collective understanding of what we live for. That understanding is sufficient, and far from undermining its sufficiency, postmodern thought tells us that we have grounds enough for action and justified condemnation in the democratic ideals we embrace, without grasping for the empty rhetoric of universal absolutes to which all subscribe but which all define differently.

But of course it's not really postmodernism that people are bothered by. It's the idea that our adversaries have emerged not from some primordial darkness, but from a history that has equipped them with reasons and motives and even with a perverted version of some virtues. Bill Maher, Dinesh D'Souza and Susan Sontag have gotten into trouble by pointing out that "cowardly" is not the word to describe men who sacrifice themselves for a cause they believe in.

Ms. Sontag grants them courage, which she is careful to say is a "morally neutral" term, a quality someone can display in the performance of a bad act. (Milton's Satan is the best literary example.) You don't condone that act because you describe it accurately. In fact, you put yourself in a better position to respond to it by taking its true measure. Making the enemy smaller than he is blinds us to the danger he presents and gives him the advantage that comes along with having been underestimated.

That is why what Edward Said has called "false universals" should be rejected: they stand in the way of useful thinking. How many times have we heard these new mantras: "We have seen the face of evil"; "these are irrational madmen"; "we are at war against international terrorism." Each is at once inaccurate and unhelpful. We have not seen the face of evil; we have seen the face of an enemy who comes at us with a full roster of grievances, goals and strategies. If we reduce that enemy to "evil," we conjure up a shape- shifting demon, a wild-card moral anarchist beyond our comprehension and therefore beyond the reach of any counterstrategies.

The same reduction occurs when we imagine the enemy as "irrational." Irrational actors are by definition without rhyme or reason, and there's no point in reasoning about them on the way to fighting them. The better course is to think of these men as bearers of a rationality we reject because its goal is our destruction. If we take the trouble to understand that rationality, we might have a better chance of figuring out what its adherents will do next and preventing it.

And "international terrorism" does not adequately describe what we are up against. Terrorism is the name of a style of warfare in service of a cause. It is the cause, and the passions informing it, that confront us. Focusing on something called international terrorism — detached from any specific purposeful agenda — only confuses matters. This should have been evident when President Vladimir Putin of Russia insisted that any war against international terrorism must have as one of its objectives victory against the rebels in Chechnya.

When Reuters decided to be careful about using the word "terrorism" because, according to its news director, one man's terrorist is another man's freedom fighter, Martin Kaplan, associate dean of the Annenberg School for Communication at the University of Southern California, castigated what he saw as one more instance of cultural relativism. But Reuters is simply recognizing how unhelpful the word is, because it prevents us from making distinctions that would allow us to get a better picture of where we are and what we might do. If you think of yourself as the target of terrorism with a capital T, your opponent is everywhere and nowhere. But if you think of yourself as the target of a terrorist who comes from somewhere, even if he operates internationally, you can at least try to anticipate his future assaults.

Is this the end of relativism? If by relativism one means a cast of mind that renders you unable to prefer your own convictions to those of your adversary, then relativism could hardly end because it never began. Our convictions are by definition preferred; that's what makes them our convictions. Relativizing them is neither an option nor a danger.

But if by relativism one means the practice of putting yourself in your adversary's shoes, not in order to wear them as your own but in order to have some understanding (far short of approval) of why someone else might want to wear them, then relativism will not and should not end, because it is simply another name for serious thought.


Stanley Fish, dean of the college of liberal arts and sciences at the University of Illinois at Chicago, is the author, most recently, of "How Milton Works."

Daniel and all,

I wrote this after your comments about using story to convey my point about mystery, let me know what you think.

John

Josef grew up surrounded by beauty and plenty. Yet there was something that seemed odd about this place. Why did he and his family wear different clothes than the other people in his neighborhood? Why did they speak a different language in their house than on the street? Why did they always have to pray to an unknown God to change things? There was nothing about Josef’s life that seemed to fit. Wherever he went he felt uncomfortable. Many times people had spit on him and called him horrible names. His father, a gravedigger came home tired and angry everyday. What was wrong with this place? Why didn’t Josef fit in? He tried to be liked by the kids in school but they always made fun of his clothes or the way he said certain words. He was beginning to hate his life, his family, himself. Every week in religion class he listened to stories about how great his people used to be. But these stories were myths for him; there was nothing in his life that connected to them. Yet, when he listened there was something deep inside that stirred? This sensation was quickly quenched as he walked home after classes.
The stories he had listened to since he was young were beginning to fade. He had no way to make sense of them. The world of these stories could not be real because if it was why was his life so horrible. The stories gave Josef a sense of emptiness. If the God his father always spoke about was so strong and so powerful why was his father and family looked down upon by everyone else around them. Who was Josef and where did he come from? Nothing in any of these stories made any sense of the world he lived in. Where was this land of milk and honey? Where were the armies that once roamed the hills destroying every enemy they faced? Then one day Josef heard a new story. This story began with God creating a world out of nothing. Josef had never imagined God was that powerful. The laws and regulations he followed everyday had taught him that God was only in need of obedient behavior, nothing more nothing less. How could this God who demanded so much and did so little be the same God who created everything? Josef strained to hear the story but its incongruity confused him.
God created the heavens and the earth and people and plants and animals and everything. This was obviously a different God than Josef and his family followed. He asked a question if this was the God of the king. His teacher looked down at him over his glasses with a soft smile and said no, this was the same God that they had talked about yesterday. This was only a new story his teacher had written the night before. He was using the class as a test to see if it made any sense. The mystery of how God could do all these things overwhelmed Josef. The stories he was used to hearing all made sense they were logical and simple this one was confusing and uneasy to hear. Josef’s question had opened the gates; from every mouth came a word of doubt. This was the king’s God it had to be because the king was the only one strong enough to have such a powerful God. If Josef’s God was this powerful wouldn’t he be better off? The teacher quickly quieted the class and returned to a familiar story.
As Josef walked home there was something different in the world that surrounded him. Things did not look so dark and forbidding, but why, he couldn’t figure it out. Through dinner and into bedtime the questions swirled in his head. If this God, his God, created all things from nothing why couldn’t he deliver Josef from his life of drudgery and pain? The next morning he ask his mother about this God. His questions seemed to make her nervous and she insisted he stop talking that way. She wanted to know where he had heard such a story. Josef told her it was the religious teacher that had told him about this God. His mother quickly retrieved Josef’s father. He ask Josef about the story again and angrily slammed his fist on the table. He told Josef he was not to go back to the class again and that he would speak to the other men in the neighborhood about this teacher. Josef got scared that he was in trouble, had he said something that was bad? As Josef walked home from school he saw a crowd gathered in the square and a man on the ground. His clothes were covered in blood and his body was twitching. As Josef got closer he realized it was his teacher, he turned and ran in fear. Why had the men in the village done this to this harmless old man?
For days Josef sat in quiet fear, not daring to ask his parents why that terrible thing had happened. After a week he finally ask his father at dinner about the teacher. His father quickly answered that the teacher was dead, guilty of lies against God. His father told Josef, people like the teacher try to stir the minds of the young people to rebel against their parents. The rebellion would eventually result in the entire community being punished by the king and his god. Too many questions from them about why things weren’t different were answered with the king’s violence. Josef learned quickly not to ask questions, look what it had done for the teacher. Josef fell into a routine again, school, work, sleep.
The years past quickly and Josef found himself at the point of making a decision about a career. His mother had always told him that he should teach religion at the synagogue. His father wanted him to be a cabinetmaker; a good solid living could be had through such a career. Josef decided to become a teacher. He left home and attended a training institute and eventually returned home to become the village’s teacher of religion. After his teacher had been killed fewer children had been attending the religious school. It was Josef’s plan to rebuild the school in hopes of generating more income for himself and his new wife. Through all his training Josef had remembered the story his old teacher had told on that fateful day. The story had stirred something inside Josef that he could not fully suppress. Was that story the reason he had become a teacher?
The day before he was to begin in his new post Josef visited the small room behind the synagogue where he would be teaching in the morning. Somehow he had managed to add three students to the four that had already enrolled. As he rummaged through the cabinets a scroll fell from above. It was covered with years of dust and made Josef sneeze for several minutes; dust has always been a problem for him. As he removed to string that encircled the scroll he began to see the beautiful characters his old teacher used to make with his writing. The first word brought Josef to his knees. The story in the scroll was the very story that had caused his teacher to be stoned. Fear seized Josef, yet something deep inside him overcame the fear and he began to read. As he read the story of how God had created all things he was overcome with a joy that he could not contain and he danced around the tiny room laughing.
Now many years later, wiser and more intelligent the story made perfect sense. It was a powerful message for him and his people. If their God created all things they no longer needed to live in subjugation. But why had his teacher been killed for telling this story? As Josef rolled up the scroll and retied the string he was consumed with a million urges. He wanted to run in the streets and tell everyone what he had discovered. But then the image of the old man lying in a bloodied heap hit him. What was it that created such fear in his father and others to cause them to kill this gentle old man? The answer was then clear. If this story was true and God was all-powerful, more powerful that any god the king and his sorcerers could muster it meant that his people were a threat to the stable society that the king had built. Josef’s people who had so long lived without hope could not understand that this story was meant for them. It was a call to them from their God that they were never left alone.
From the beginning of time God had followed, led and loved them. They had let their own fear and doubt destroy their joy and that was the reason they lived the lives they did. That was why they had killed the teacher years before. This story was a cry in their wilderness, a cry calling them to reclaim their roots. What they had seen as dangerous God meant for good. They were unable to see the power and mystery that the story held. Too many years of living calculated controlled lives had deadened their senses to understand anything mysterious. God had become a comfortable proposition that fit into their hard and shallow lives. God did not ask much from Josef’s people did not expect much in return.
But how would Josef show his aging father and the other patriarchs that this story is intended to restore their people to their rightful place. Years of living under the thumb of an oppressive king had taught Josef and his people that they were not to question anything. And here was God telling Josef and his people that there are a called out group, a chosen seed that God would once again enable and empower. Josef wanted to scream the message he had learned from the rooftops but something held him back. As he contemplated what to do it became clear. He would secretly share the story with friends and trusted associates until there joy and desire to claim their heritage would grow into a force the village and its people could not ignore. Josef did succeed in his task and his people through the help of this story reclaimed their place in history.
Yet as I tell this story we are like the men who stoned the old teacher. We live in fear of changing; comfortable with the world God has “given” us. But the truth is that God is telling new stories and some of us are listening seeking to find a way to embrace the mystery that change Josef’s world. That same spirit of embrace must prevail in our world or we are destined to stone all our old teachers and live lives filled with fear and doubt. God wants us to rediscover who we are and what we were meant to be and embracing mystery is an essential part of that journey.

John,

awesome. f**king awesome.

i wondered to myself if i should post my comments here or somewhere else, but from your discussion on criticism on faithmaps and the fact that this is primarily a site of friends, i decided to post them here.

only two things, really. i would continue to develop the imagery in the story. what setting is it? dirt floors? stone floors? wood floors? carpeted floors? or for us to decide? you get the idea. paint just a little more with your words. give us just enough to really become absorbed in the story. for example, your part about sneezing and how dust had always been a problem for Josef was really great! (also just the spelling of the name Josef conjuered a particular look and time for me.)

also, i would leave the ending a little more vague. the meaning of the story is clear. here is my suggestion (first sentence from you for context {w/ 1 editorial correction :) }):

"He would secretly share the story with friends and trusted associates until their joy and desire to claim their heritage would grow into a force the village and its people could not ignore. He would allow God to tell this new story, and others, too. And he would tell his stories to anyone who cared to listen, to seek a way to embrace the mystery that had so radically changed his world."

full stop. also i would start a new paragraph with that section and use Josef instead of 'he' on that first sentence (if you were to use this) because i realized that was too many 'he's' all together.

more positives: loved the martyr aspect to the story. loved the characters. at times i can relate to each character in this story.

this is the beauty of stories. you don't say what's right and what's wrong. you allow the conscience of the reader make the decision for him/her. you completely allow their context to affect their meanings, but at the same time you really are having a great deal of influence.

fantastic.

please if anyone else would like to comment, don't hesitate. if you aren't a part of our contributor's team, just email me. the email address is somewhere on this page (sorry i'm too lazy to type the tag!) :P.

cheers, daniel

Josef grew up surrounded by beauty and plenty. Yet there was something that seemed odd about this place. Why did he and his family wear different clothes than the other people in his neighborhood? Why did they speak a different language in their home than on the street? Why did they always have to pray to an unknown God to change things? There was nothing about Josef’s life that seemed to fit. Wherever he went he felt uncomfortable. Many times people had spit on him and called him horrible names. His father, a gravedigger came home tired and angry every night. What was wrong with this place? Why didn’t Josef fit in? He tried to be liked by the kids in school but they always made fun of his clothes or the way he said certain words. He was beginning to hate his life, his family, himself. Every week in religion class he listened to stories about how great his people used to be. But these stories were myths for him; there was nothing in his life that connected to them. Yet, when he listened there was something deep inside that stirred? This sensation was quickly quenched as he walked through the stone covered streets on his way home.
The stories he had listened to since he was young were beginning to fade. He had no way to make sense of them. The world of these stories could not be real because if it was why was his life so horrible. The stories gave Josef a sense of emptiness. If the God his father always spoke about was so strong and so powerful why was his father and family looked down upon by everyone else around them. Why did they live in a house that had only one window and dirt floors. Josef’s school mates whose fathers hard jobs in the city had houses with courtyards and tile on their floors. Certainly the God that Josef and his family prayed to was incapable of helping him. Who was Josef and where did he come from? Nothing in any of these stories made any sense of the world he lived in. Where was this land of milk and honey? Where were the armies that once roamed the hills destroying every enemy they faced? Then one day Josef heard a new story. This story began with God creating a world out of nothing. Josef had never imagined God was that powerful. The laws and regulations he followed everyday had taught him that God was only in need of obedient behavior, nothing more nothing less. How could this God who demanded so much and did so little be the same God who created everything? Josef strained to hear the story but its incongruity confused him.
God created the heavens and the earth and people and plants and animals and everything. This was obviously a different God than Josef knew. He asked a question if this was the god of the king. His teacher looked down at him over his glasses with a soft smile and said no, this was the same God that they had talked about yesterday. This was only a new story his teacher had written the night before. He was using the class as a test to see if it made any sense. Many times before the teacher had told them new stories but they all spoke about what clothes to wear or what food to eat. This new story was different, almost magical.
The mystery of how God could do all these things overwhelmed Josef. The stories he was used to hearing all made sense they were logical and simple this one was confusing and uneasy to hear. Josef’s question had opened the gates; from every mouth came a word of doubt. Most of the kids were laughing at the teacher. God could not create anything as big and amazing as the world. The king’s god and his helpers had built the world. Great beast held up the world and powerful demons caused the storms to punish the people for their failures. Josef had learned long ago that the gods of the king punished people who did not do what they were told. This was the king’s god it had to be because the king was the only one strong enough to have such a powerful god. If Josef’s God was this powerful wouldn’t he be better off? The children in the class had become restless. Was their teacher, a man who never questioned anything starting to become dangerous? The children began to talk with each other and a sense of fear began to fill the room. The teacher quickly quieted the class and returned to a familiar story.
As Josef walked home there was something different in the world that surrounded him. Things did not look so dark and forbidding, but why, he couldn’t figure it out. Through dinner and into bedtime the questions swirled in his head. As he climbed the ladder to his bed he hesitated, should he tell his father what he had heard, but fear once again seized him and he quickly threw himself into bed. The usual hard mat seemed more uncomfortable that normal as Josef struggled with the story.
If this God, his God, created all things from nothing why couldn’t he deliver Josef from his life of drudgery and pain? The next morning as his mother stooped over the fire stirring a the breakfast porridge, he ask her about this God. His questions seemed to make her nervous and she insisted he stop talking that way. She angrily ask him where he had heard such a story, who had filled his head with such lies. Josef told her it was the religious teacher. His mother quickly retrieved Josef’s father. He ask Josef about the story again and angrily slammed his fist on the table knocking the wooden bowls of steaming porridge to the floor. He told Josef he was not to go back to the class again and that he would speak to the other men in the village about this teacher. Josef got scared that he was in trouble, had he said something that was bad?
As Josef walked home from school a cool wind blew shutters crashed into the white washed walls that bordered the street. As he turned the corner he saw a crowd gathered in the square and a man on the ground. His clothes were covered in blood and his body was twitching. As Josef got closer he realized it was his teacher, he turned and ran in fear. Why had the men in the village done this to this harmless old man? Would Josef be next?
For days Josef sat in quiet fear, not daring to ask his parents why that terrible thing had happened. After a week he finally ask his father at dinner about the teacher. His father quickly answered that the teacher was dead, guilty of lies against God. With his dirt stained fingers cradling his head he slowly put his feet on the table and leaned back. Josef’s mother protested the feet but a quick growl returned her to her place at the sink. He told Josef, people like the teacher try to stir the minds of the young people to rebel against their parents. That rebellion would eventually result in the entire village being punished by the king and his god. Too many questions from them about why things weren’t different were answered with the king’s violence. Josef’s father told him of a man that questioned things when he was child. This man who told different stories was eventually killed by the king. Josef learned quickly not to ask questions, look what it had done for the teacher. Josef fell into a routine again, school, work, sleep.
The years past quickly and Josef found himself at the point of making a decision about a career. His mother had always told him that he should teach religion at the synagogue. His father wanted him to be a cabinetmaker; a good solid living could be had through such a career. Josef decided to become a teacher. He left home and attended a training institute and eventually returned home to become the village’s teacher of religion. After his teacher had been killed the school had slowly died. It was Josef’s plan to rebuild the school in hopes of generating more income for himself and his new wife. Through all his training Josef had remembered the story his old teacher had told on that fateful day. The story had stirred something inside Josef that he could not fully suppress. Was that story the reason he had become a teacher?
The day before he was to begin his new job Josef visited the small room behind the synagogue where he had the story. Somehow he had managed to add three students to the four that had already enrolled. As he rummaged through the cabinets a scroll fell from above. It was covered with years of dust and made Josef sneeze for several minutes; dust has always been a problem for him. As he removed to string that encircled the scroll he began to see the beautiful characters his old teacher used to make with his writing. The first word brought Josef to his knees. The story in the scroll was the very story that had caused his teacher to be stoned. Fear seized Josef, yet something deep inside him overcame the fear and he began to read. As he read the story of how God had created all things he was overcome with a joy that he could not contain and he danced around the tiny room laughing.
Now many years later, wiser and more intelligent the story made perfect sense. It was a powerful message for him and his people. If their God created all things they no longer needed to live in subjugation. But why had his teacher been killed for telling this story? As Josef rolled up the scroll and retied the string he was consumed with a million urges. He wanted to run in the streets and tell everyone what he had discovered. But the image of the old man lying in a bloodied heap hit him. What was it that created such fear in his father and others to cause them to kill the gentle old man? The answer was then clear. If this story was true and God was all-powerful, more powerful that any god the king and his sorcerers could muster it meant that his people were a threat to the stable society that the king had built. Josef’s people who had so long lived without hope could not understand that this story was meant for them. It was a call to them from their God that they were never left alone.
From the beginning of time God had followed, led and loved them. They had let their own fear and doubt destroy their joy. That was why they had killed the teacher years before. This story was a cry in their wilderness, a cry calling them to reclaim their roots. What they had seen as dangerous God meant for good. They were unable to see the power and mystery that the story held. Too many years of living calculated controlled lives had deadened their senses to understand anything mysterious. God had become a comfortable proposition that fit into their hard and shallow lives.
But how would Josef show his aging father and the other patriarchs that this story is intended to restore their people to their rightful place. Years of living under the thumb of an oppressive king had taught Josef and his people that they were not to question anything. And here was God telling Josef and his people that there are a called out group, a chosen seed that God would once again enable and empower. Josef wanted to scream the message he had learned from the rooftops but something held him back. As he contemplated what to do it became clear.
Josef would secretly share the story with friends and trusted associates until their joy and desire to claim their heritage would grow into a force the village and its people could not ignore. He would allow God to tell this new story, and others, too. And he would tell his stories to anyone who cared to listen, to seek a way to embrace the mystery that had so radically changed his world.

Had an idea for the "next" "installation" (for lack of a better word). Funny how I'm never explicitly thinking of these things but they just come.

aside: I'm so sick of alt. worship already. You'll see: it'll be the next big thing in Christendom and swallowed whole, digested, and turned into shit by the church-at-large.

At Vaux they did this thing (actually I thought of this then, but just now I heard Miriam talking to D about the movie "Babette's Feast" and it made me think of it again) where they showed the movie, which is based on a little story, while somebody told the story to us. It was like getting two stories at once, but really they were the same story.

Also there's that "layered narrative" concept....

Also I'm reading that book "Miss Wyoming" where each chapter is from a different time in one of the 2 characters' lives. There's about 6 threads, so about every 6 chapters you're picking up linearly from about 6 chapters ago. Anyway, there's one or two threads that I find more interesting that others, so every time I get to one of those chapters it's really exciting.

Which gave birth to my third will-I-ever-actually-write-one book idea, which I told S about last weekend, which is to write a bunch of loosely knit self-contained short stories and compile them in some random way. Sort of just like Miss Wyoming but each chapter totally self contained and the interconnectedness not so clear until maybe you read the last chapter. Just nonlinear story on a few growth hormones.

So after all that background, my idea is....to do a layered narrative night. Have like 10 stories going on at once, and people experience them all simulateously, except they will only be able to process a few at a time, and will be naturally drawn to only one or two.

The other thing that made me think of this was the night I was at hate mail reading my book there, and I had two conversations going on either side of me, so I was following three stories at once. It was pretty cul. I wrote about it on the hate mail weblog. http://hatemail.blogspot.com (November 17, 2001)

So the question is how do you pull this off?

Couple movies. That's easy. Books would be hard because it's an active thing; maybe if everyone carried around one book and read it during. Live theater would be awesome, but then somebody(s) would have to do it, and they might not experience the event then (not to mention we only have about 7 people interested in this type of thing anyway). Or, like Calvin's thingie, everyone could be part of the theater....

So I was thinking between Calvin's murder mystery night, ya'll's plans and interest in story, and my thoughts on doing a super smashed-story non-linear night with a couple of common threads, we could come up with something. So bark back yer thoughts. When/how/where?

I'll keep this as anon as possible, but something about this rubs me weird. Please clue me in if I'm totally off base here. But my comment from post prior about shit came from reading this kind of thing. Because my experience with spirituality in London has been the highlight of my journey so far. And it doesn't matter, in fact it's better, that there aren't so many goups or people doing it. It's intensity is actually better for it. Anyway:

"On Saturday I arrived in London. Here I will be for just over a
week. The research we do consists of doing interviews, visiting churches,
meeting pastors, praying and collecting all sorts of data: pictures,
magazines, quotes, etc.
"London has 7.1 million people. Less than 5% attends church on
Sunday. Of the people in ages 20 - 35, even fewer attend church. Yet this
group cites loneliness and relational difficulties as its main problem.

"It's not that we don't see any great churches in London. Last
Sunday I visit Ichthus City Gates, which meets in the London Comedy club.
Where artists of all kinds poke fun at everything they know (God and the
church frequently being the but of the jokes, of course), around 150 young
people worship God and seek him on Sunday morning. A happening place indeed
(see pictures).
"So London has some really good churches that reach out. Problem
is, there are so few of them! They are hardly scratching the surface! We
could use, not just one, but a thousand more churches like City Gates! Which
is why we think London is a good location to plant a church! And then
another one. And another one. And…

"IMPACT
"{our} vision is to contribute toward making an impact for Christ
in Europe. We do this through leadership development, and church planting.
Our desire is to see a movement of new churches spread out over Europe,
impacting the continent in every way. To this end we are endeavoring to
plant some 50 churches by the year 2010.
"{we} have an active and significant role in this, and we
appreciate your prayer and support for this!"

what is alt.worship? this strikes the heart.

"i have a thing about this ageism, it seems so prevalent in evangelical
thinking, the emphasis on 'young people' having all the answers. it's partly
modernist - new=better, and that 'generations' thing that evangelicals do.
it's partly a failure to realise that it's cultural position that matters.
in our context, the young/old boundary that matters is the modern/postmodern
one loosely speaking, which can perhaps be taken as the pre/post 60s
boundary as well - which is moving further and further up the age scale. the
boundary is now around age 45, but the church as usual hasn't quite caught
up with this, because it created whole patterns of ministry in the 70s when
this boundary was around 25. looking around it seems that most of the people
on the cutting edge are actually in their 30s, partly because it takes a
while to develop the knowledge and self-confidence [and self-knowledge] to
critique the church effectively from within. and also because it's only when
you move beyond the exciting relevant world of youth ministry that the real
plight of the church hits you. sitting in a pew age 30, listening to a
boring sermon, remembering how much better it was when your youth group did
its own worship and wondering why you can't play your own music in church
like you did then..."
Steve Collins

Small Ritual - QuietComfort Church

HOUSE2HOUSE ARTICLE

Three years ago my wife, Lisa, our three kids and I moved from Northern
Minnesota to San Francisco to “start a house church movement” among what we
use to call “Urban Post-modern peoples.” We bought an old Victorian house in
the Mission District-- an eclectic multicultural neighborhood including Latin
American Immigrants, old Hippies, Punk Rockers, Bohemian Marxists, Art
Activists, young gay men, lesbians, and Dot Com hipsters. We chose San
Francisco because of it long-standing reputation as the most progressive,
postmodern and post-Christian city in America. This is where we wanted to
work out faith and Christian community.

We had every hope that this venture would be successful. I had just finished
a three-year stint working with students at a traditional church. They hated
my best efforts at the classic youth ministry “thang”-the wanging electric
guitar worship songs and comedy a la hardcore-gospel-message routine. There
was a disconnect between the realities of their lives and the culture-bound
practices of our church. I invited them to come to our house, “the shack”
on Sunday nights. It started with Lisa and I and three students around a
campfire in the woods behind our house. Eventually Sunday night “Rocks”
replaced the youth program at the church. More and more students started
showing up with their non-believing friends. Our living room was packed with
skate kids, punk rockers, hippie girls, sports and band people from seven
different high schools. Everything we did was participatory and experiential.
I led behind the scenes and mentored 5 or 6 senior high students who
facilitated most of the meetings. We had lively discussions on pop culture,
sexuality, death, philosophy, and the Bible. We worshipped. We prayed. We
celebrated communion. We had ministry times where people would pray and speak
prophetically over one another. The Holy Spirit manifested in some amazing
ways! Students, who had never been to a church, were showing up with friends
and engaging in our discussions, prayer and worship. They sensed a warm and
different vibe. After six or eight months they would come to me and say,
“Mark, what is this I hear about being saved?”

We looked hard at the early chapters of the book of Acts and Paul’s writing
on the fellowship of believers-and gradually came to the realization that
this ragamuffin crew of kids was fulfilling New Testament functions of a
church.

Our very first experience leading a relationally-based group happened five
years earlier. Back then Lisa and I spent our time working with children and
families in low-income housing projects. It grieved us to see that, when
these Jerry Springer style families found faith, they didn’t feel comfortable
or accepted in our middle-class church. We also drooled over Acts 2:42-47
where the church seemed like an empowered dynamic of relationships rather
than a culturally bound institution. We wanted a simple and informal
alternative to the highly organized form of the typical church. We invited
two other couples to join us on Sunday nights to experiment with “New
Testament church life.” Within two months there were 30 of us meeting
including 10 kids! It was a great mix of professionals, working class people
AND families from the projects. We ate meals together, met for worship and
communion, and served one another in practical ways. It was a great
experiment! I was only 22, and was not prepared for the level of leadership
and organization required to sustain such a group. So after a summer break we
didn’t call the group back together. However, this experience gave us
confidence that we could potentially catalyze relationally-based Christian
community.

Roll forward to San Francisco 1998. In the first six months two house
churches formed with 10-12 people per group. People were excited about the
dream of true community and the possibility of seeing a faith movement happen
in San Francisco birthed from these house churches. But quickly things got
weird…

Our group had a tendency to react to the traditional American expressions of
the church. Many of us had never felt accepted by the church or had been hurt
by church relationships. A lot of us were idealists who longed for the
reclamation of the church in the book of Acts and its redemptive potential in
society. And each of us faced some internal and external criticism for our
decision to leave the dominant church paradigm. And, lets face it, it takes a
lot more commitment and participation to be in a house church than an
audience-based worship service. This led some of us to develop a rather
dogmatic “Biblical” rationale for the structure and functioning of our
church. If we were giving our lives to this, and it was costing us so much
time and energy, then it had better be the “right” way. We spent a lot of
energy reinforcing and defending our position and “disciplining” those who
weren’t as ardent or committed to the model.

We were never able to settle issues of leadership and organization. All of us
somewhat fit the sterotypical psychic profile of Generation X. Most of us
grew up in single parent households where we learned to distrust authority
and take care of ourselves. Others of us were reacting to over-authoritarian
religious upbringings. Consequently we feared and avoided anything resembling
order, planning, organization or directive leadership. It had to be
spontaneous and organic. There could be no leadership beyond the collective
consensus. This made it extremely difficult for us to make decisions or take
action.

Sometimes the people who need a sense of Christian community the most are
least equipped for community. We weren’t prepared for the level of brokeness
among the people in our church. Most of us wrestled with depression,
addiction recovery, gender and sexuality issues, relational tensions, or some
type of mental disorder-issues common to the population of San Francisco.
Everyone made valiant efforts to seek God and love one another. But, as a
group, we simply didn’t have the collective energy to function sustainably in
a healthy way. In our immature or misguided efforts to love, we often hurt
one another.

Our church stayed together for more than two years. There were some beautiful
moments along the way: Courageous acts or love and self-sacrifice; care for
the poor and marginalized; generosity; people sharing living space;
opportunities to witness and tender moments of affection--and lots of
memories: retreats; camping trips; hikes, long talks, hundreds of meals, etc.
We did some things as a church that we hadn’t seen or done before. We tried
and did the best we could with the insight and resources we had.

One of the more painful disappointments was the unrealized dream of seeing
our generation experience life with God. We sincerely thought that reforming
the structure and functions of the church would unleash the power of the New
Testament church. In retrospect I believe our house church ambitions were a
premature resolution to larger issues facing the people of God in the West.

We live in a society that is increasingly secular, post-Christian and
postmodern-dramatically different from the cultural ethos and social
structures that allowed for the expansion and maintenance of a Christian
movement in the modern era. This cultural shift has prompted many of us to
deconstruct our modernist American conservative evangelicalism. The modernist
version of life with God simply doesn’t work for us-in form or content. We
thought that merely changing the form of the church would adequately address
the challenges of an emerging postmodern era. But the deconstruction and
eventual reconstruction must be more fundamental. I’m convinced that a
proactive movement of faith will not come from this deconstructive phase or a
superficial reconstruction. Nor will it come from a preoccupation with
maintaining “spiritual” market shares through pragmatic repackaging and
image management of old time modernist Christianity.

I did not avoid the temptation to use relationally-based multiplication
models as a utilitarian alternative to failing church growth technologies. I
wanted to be successful. We moved to San Francisco without adequate
sensitivity to varying spiritual atmospheres. Our positive house church
experiences in the Midwest developed in a culture bathed in a Judeo-Christian
ethos. As a pastor I had positional credibility, language and cultural
fluency and a ready audience eager for what I had to offer. People were
looking for the “Uber-Spiritual” experience of a house church. We were
naïve to think that the same approach would be effective in a thoroughly
post-Christian environment-with a population generally hostile to Christian
faith.

In our quest for the recovery of transforming life, we hadn’t gone far back
enough. We were still coming up with answers that only work for people still
in the shadow of a modernist Christian culture. We went to the book of Acts
for a prescriptive solution. But the events in the book of Acts were prompted
by a compelling primal reality-Jesus. We needed to go back to the gospels and
rediscover the goodness and beauty of the kingdom of God.

Jesus is the place where reconstruction begins. One time a guy in a tattoo
shop told me, “Jesus is cool. Its just that the church has F%#$ed with Jesus.
I think Christianity was at its best when it was secret, hidden and you could
die for it.” Jesus came announcing “the Kingdom of God is at hand” His good
news was that the creator is caring, present and now active bringing all of
life back into the Genesis vision. We have begun to embrace Jesus not only
as savior but also teacher. Jesus makes the way to life and shows us how to
live life in the kingdom of God.

In our exuberance to see a faith movement happen in our generation, we
confused the questions. We started by asking “What form(s) should the church
take in this emerging culture?” When we needed to be asking, “What does the
life and teaching of Jesus mean in this time and place?” We made the church
the kingdom instead of seeking the kingdom as the people of God.

For now our family is exploring a primal pursuit of Jesus and his kingdom. We
are acknowledging that we know a lot less than we thought we knew. We have a
loose network of fellow postmodern pilgrim friends also working out life with
God in San Francisco. Our gatherings are about practicing and imitating
Jesus’ life in our neighborhoods: eating with the homeless, creating art,
engaging in classic spiritual disciplines, practicing hospitality, etc. Our
vision has changed from a house church movement to an indigenous kingdom
movement.

Awhile back we developed the following identity statement:

“We are a movement of people in San Francisco, who, believing that our city
is loved by God, seek a way of life that reverberates with the goodness and
beauty of the kingdom of God.”

Mark Scandrette lives with his family in the Mission District of San
Francisco. He is cofounder of Re/Imagine!, an incubator for Kingdom Living in
San Francisco. He can be reached at Scandrette@aol.com.

Re/IMAGINE!
Mark A. Scandrette

Christmas eve-eve pot-luck litergy

Sunday, December 23, 2001, 9pm

at the Gravatt home:
4365 SW 10th Pl, apt. 104, Deerfield Beach, Fl, 33442

Here are the rules:

You bring the service with you. Bring one or two elements of worship that you specifically connect with and be ready to implement them in the above setting (an apartment living room). If it just sits there, then bring everything it needs to sit there. If it is more overtly participatory, be prepared to faciliate that participation. Don't take our whole night, but we will be otherwise very tolerant of form and function.

There are always those people that come to pot-lucks w/o bringing any food. Sometimes they grab something really quick at Publix on their way over. If you find yourself doing this, then at least buy the organic chips, if you know what I mean.

On the subject of literal food, feel free to bring something. But only after you've prepared your portion of the service. Cuz w/o you all, there will be no service! Get it? Good.

Please email me with your rsvp and general needs/time frame for the service.

Cheers,

From Time Magazine, Dec. 17th, 2001 issue, p.60-61 "God as a Postmodern" by
David van Biema

"Christian faith has long endured the chipping away at authority that is
the hallmark of modern academia. But church and campus have never seemed as
estranged as they have since the advent of postmodernism, the notion that
there is no universal truth, merely competing "narratives" jockeying
eternally for supremacy. That is, until 1990, when a young British professor
named John Milbank pioneered what "The Chronicle of Higher Education" has
suggested may be the "biggest development in theology since Martin Luther
nailed his 95 theses to the church door."
Milbank opted to cite (if not love) his enemy. By exposing the nihilism
of secular thought, he writes, postmodernism freed Christian theology from
the need to "measure up to ... standards of scientific truth and normative
rationality." Thus unburdened, he suggests, it enjoys several advantages
over secular competitiors. Long before postmodernism, Christianity accpeted
unknowableness as part of God's nature. The Christian "story" is dense with
associations and can be applied universally. In fact, he writes, its central
dynamic of ever unfolding divine love places it outside - and above -
postmodernism's conviction that each contending world view is rooted in and
sustained by violence.
Milbank's conclusion: Christian theology can now reclaim its medieval
position as "queen of the sciences", before which disciplines like
sociology, philosophy and economics must bow down. He and colleagues dubbed
their movement "radical orthodoxy" and began a giddy exploration of
theology's vast, recovered social responsibilities.
The effort made them academic stars - Milbank, 49, occupies a prestigious
chair at the University of Virginia - and drew fierce fire. He notes that "I
would not expect Muslims or Buddhists to buy this wholly, at all." Even some
fellow believers find it hard to square his vision of a peaceful,
all-embracing Christianity with the religion that burned heretics and
launched crusades. Besides, not everyone is a postmodernist. Conservatives
cringe when he says that discussions of the acutal physical reality of the
resurrection have "no place" in his theology. But at the very least, he has
cleared a way for the theologians to reclaim their place at the academic
table, ending decades, if not centuries of marginalization.

at fuzzy we
pray, sit back, listen, read, talk, absorb,
are absorbed, reflect, care, laugh
discuss the bible, try to make it real in our lives, campaign
support local arts and culture; we sit quiet
we do not tend to have noel richards or matt redman or even
sing, but we worship, and most important of all
we share bread and wine with each other;
we reflect on that death and that life, we remember and we hope
in our worship we integrate concern for the global environment,
action on poor country debt
repentance, thoughts on how we are shaped
thoughts on loneliness and solitude, suffering, celebration,
wilderness and mountaintop, feast and famine, how we were created
what we should do, how we should be church together
home and family life, work life, friends and community
we are about being a community, worshipping together
not just turning up and receiving
this is why we share the bread and wine,
so that the body might not be broken
we have not given up meeting together as some have done
come along and eat (bread) drink (wine) and be fuzzy

another great column by Mr. Steve Collins!....

What if we marketed church as an aesthetic phenomenon? And then set up the kind of church experiences that non-Christians find appealing? Are we too eager to give them the Gospel content? Could we let them wallow a while in mere atmosphere, beer in hand? "A while" meaning, not "until half-way through the service", but for a month, a year. Or two.

Have we the faith, the patience? Can we scatter the clues without strings, watch them leave without getting clingy? Are we open, clearly and obviously open, to having a casual uncommitted audience for our worship? Will we let them shop without pressing them to buy? And if we charged £13.50 entry, would they think we had something worth paying to see?

Vaux is an amazing alt.worship type service in London that Miriam and I went to in October. Here is some stuff from their latest:

"

I'll leave you with something I wrote for our most recent service. We are pushing into what we have called our 'dirt season' - and as it was the beginning of Vx 4.0 - ie first service of the new year - we thought we 'd actually do a spoken intro to where we seem to be at and what we are currently thinking about - so it's not definitive or complete, but might give you something ... actually, first here's the copy from the back of the latest flyer:

Vaux, in its essence, has always been about journey. Stillness can reflect satiation, and Vaux perseveres unsatisfied, pursuing God and our doubts further into the city; exploring a spirituality that embraces the world under pressure by mining the traditions of the past and fusing them with the culture of the present.
Vaux is also about gift, and like the journey, the gift cycle between ourselves, our art and our faith must be kept moving. Where gifts stop, art becomes commerce, and worship a form of consumption. Where there is no gift, there can be no art. Our services are designed to be places of free exchange between God, ourselves and our culture.

We believe that to follow in the way of Christ demands speaking about God in ways that people understand. Using forms and language that are rich and complex, not didactic. It is our hope that our services will provide spaces where people can do what is most natural: to communicate in creative ways with the source of human life.

And the piece from last night:

Welcome to Vx 4.0 As the flyer states, Vx is about journey. Staying still can reflect satiation, satisfaction with where you are, an inertia to change and desire to keep things as they are.

Vx continues to persevere unsatisfied. We have not arrived. What we do is not it. And so this year, as in the last 3, at the heart of what we are doing is a desire to pursue God further. To not accept pat answers. To allow our doubts to surface and charge the real questions of how to forge a spirituality that works in the situation we find ourselves. One that "embraces a world under pressure by mining the traditions of the past, and fusing them with the culture of the present."

Our pursuit of God is taking us simultaneously into the depths of ourselves, and into the heart of the city. And this dual journey seems vital. That we begin to strip away the fiction, the images, the bandages that we wrap round ourselves - as if we were, as Merton says, invisible, and needed to wrap these things round us to make us seem real. To become more real to ourselves, and yet to become more integrated into a city that seems to be facing an increasing vacuum of principles, grace and community feeling.
The paradox struck me the other day in the tube: a carriage stuffed full of may be 100 people; no one able to move for arm-pits. And yet the place was empty. Nobody was actually there. Some were elsewhere in music, some in headlines and magazines; some just closed their. But not one person was actually there. Everyone in apnoea - holding their breath, refusing to interact.

We have to stop holding our breath in the city. We have to start breathing the air around, and interacting. And we have to begin to know ourselves, and admit who we are. Here lies the irony: in a world too restless to find time to change, we have to embark on a journey of stillness: finding 'Sabbath space' outside of economics and consumption and advertisements. Space to open the well of our inner-life, what ever dark water that may uncover.

So to this season of dirt.

The story is told that Carl Jung, the psychoanalyst, had a vision. He recounts in his autobiography:

"The sky was gloriously blue, the day one of radiant sunshine. The roof of a cathedral glittered, the sun sparkling from the new, brightly glazed tiles. I was overwhelmed by the beauty of the sight, and thought: "The world is beautiful, and the church is beautiful, and God made all this and sits above it all far away in the blue sky on a golden throne and" Here came a great hole in my thoughts, and a choking sensation. I felt numbed, and knew only: "Don't go on thinking now! Something terrible is coming"

"I gathered all my courage, as though I were about to leap forthwith into hell-fire, and let the thought come. I saw before me the cathedral, the blue sky. God sits on his golden throne, high above the world - and from under the throne an enormous turd falls upon the sparkling new roof, shatters it, and breaks the walls of the cathedral."

I felt an enormous, indescribable relief. Instead of the expected damnation, grace had come upon me, and I wept for happiness and gratitude."

Jung goes on to talk about how he experienced the church - where his father was minister - as lifeless. He saw his father as reliable, but powerless, presiding over a church purified to the point of sterility. And he saw Victorian Christianity as dead religion - finally blocked of any life by its own purity.

The problem of what to do with our dirt is one that still faces us. The church Jung experienced seemed to advocate hiding it, denying it, submerging it into our subconscious. And perhaps still does. 'How are you?' 'I'm fine'. Hold your breath, and deny you are even here.

Dirt, of course, comes in many forms. It has been defined by one as "matter out of place". Soil on our shoes is dirt. But a discarded crisp packet in newly ploughed soil is dirt. And food that 2 seconds ago was delicious, becomes repugnant as it is scraped onto another plate. If you feel out of place, you can feel excluded, dirty.

But dirt has also been described as that which has no place. When we have finished making sense of our world, dirt is the stuff that doesn't fit. Other races, women priests, homosexuals, the infected, the disabled, the asylum seeker. Dirt is always a product of creating order. And where there is dirt, there is always a system of some kind, and rules about dirt are meant to preserve that system. Honouring the distinction between the dirty and the clean helps make the world an orderly place, while dishonouring that distinction - defecating in the wrong place, or mixing with the infected - threatens the design, the cosmos.

The book from which we read about Jung is 'Trickster makes this world' by Lewis Hyde. In it he analyses the trickster figures from various mythologies around the world, and uses their stories to argue that it is these dodgy characters that invigorate life, and bridge the gap between heaven and earth.

'What tricksters in general like to do', he says, 'is to erase or violate that line between the dirty and the clean. As a rule, trickster takes a god who lives on high and debases him or her with earthly dirt, or at least appears to debase - for in fact the usual consequence of this dirtying is the god's eventual renewal.'

So we come to Christ, who stamped over the line of clean and dirty, ate with the prostitutes, touched the lepers, raised the dead, drank with extortionists, walked with foreigners.

And as we see dirt as 'matter out of place', we see Christ on the cross as the final picture of this: become dirt for us. For what could be more out of place, more wrong than God dying, cursed as a criminal.

And as we see tricksters as the figures who debase gods to renew them, we see Christ as the ultimate trickster: debasing himself, descending into the filth of hell and death, in order to bring renewal.

So we must ask ourselves, as Jung's vision forces us to: have we overpurified Christ, left no place for him to interact with the dirt that was his everyday life? Have we made the church too like the Pharisees that he denounced as 'whitewashed tombs' - quick to judge people as dirty, and quicker to alienate them from the place of cleansing?

Have we closed the spiritual doors to tightly on those parts of our journey that didn't fit with the nice, cosy order. The dark nights that caused us pain, that made us suffer?

If we are to re-invigorate the church, to make it again the body of Christ, we must allow it to become filthy. To accept all who come into the doors without judging. And as we will come to communion later and proclaim that we, individually as well as corporately, are the body of Christ, we must begin to accept that most difficult and dirty of people: ourselves.

So welcome to the dirt season. Part of this continuing journey into ourselves, and into the city. We hope you will travel with us over the year.

"

Inight Home Joan Jeanrenaud, formerly of the Kronos Quartet, is doing a short tour in England during February & March with what looks like an interesting programme involving video art as well as music. There seems to be a certain strain of religious thought running through the pieces.

"Throughout the performance, Joan explores new ways of creating and performing music, inspired by visions of the future and emotions of the past. Mark Grey's 'Blood Red' uses her Zeta electric cello linked to realtime computer sampling, while her own work 'Altarpiece' was written in response to an array of altars seen on the Day of the Dead.

Metamorphosis also celebrates long time associations and friendships. At the core of the evening are sections of Philip Glass' intense, romantic, masterpiece 'Metamorphosis' illuminated by three video artists who create a stunning triptych of images linked to Joan's performance. Joan draws particular inspiration for her work from the Fluxus art movement, represented by Yoko Ono's 'Lighting Piece' and 'Wood Piece'. These works inform the new visual counterpoint created in works by Karen Tanaka ('The Song of Songs') and Steve Mackey ('Cairn'), and a new arrangement of Hamza El Din's gorgeous cycle 'Escalay' (Waterwheel) which she first played on the classic Kronos album 'Pieces of Africa' "
